# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

This section is calibrated to the **KTU 2024 End-Semester Examination (ESE)** pattern: Part A short answers (3 marks each) and Part B long answers (14 marks each, with internal choice).

---

## 5.1 Part A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper Set B]**  
> *Define Non-Destructive Testing. List any four commonly used NDT methods.*

**Model Answer (target: 3 marks / ~80 words):**

> **Non-Destructive Testing (NDT)** is a group of analytical techniques used to evaluate the integrity, properties, or composition of a material or component **without impairing its future usefulness**. It is central to the forensic engineering tool box because it preserves the artifact as evidence.
>
> Four commonly used NDT methods are:
> 1. **Visual Testing (VT)**
> 2. **Dye Penetrant Testing (DPT)**
> 3. **Ultrasonic Testing (UT)**
> 4. **Radiographic Testing (RT)**
>
> *(Other acceptable: MPT, ET, AET, IRT.)*

*(1 mark for the correct definition • 1 mark for grouping as "evidence-preserving" • 1 mark for any four correctly named methods.)*

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**  
> *Differentiate between Destructive Testing (DT) and Non-Destructive Testing (NDT) with one forensic-engineering example each.*

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Destructive Testing (DT) | Non-Destructive Testing (NDT) |
|--------|--------------------------|-------------------------------|
| Specimen after test | Destroyed / consumed | Fully intact, reusable |
| Purpose | Quantitative mechanical properties | Defect detection, sizing, mapping |
| Position in tool box | Last, after NDT localisation | First, evidence-preserving |
| Example | Tensile test of a cut coupon from a failed shaft | Dye penetrant scan of a pressure-vessel nozzle weld |

*(1 mark per row of the table, or 1 mark for the contrast + 1 mark each for the two examples = 3 marks.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Long-Answer Questions (Internal Choice)

> **Q3(A). [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module-3 slot]** *(14 marks)*  
> *(a)* Classify the principal NDT methods into surface, volumetric, and dynamic categories. For each category, name two methods and state the physical principle exploited. *(7 marks)*  
> *(b)* A 10 mm thick mild-steel plate is inspected by a 4 MHz normal-beam ultrasonic probe. The longitudinal wave speed in steel is 5900 m/s. A flaw echo is observed at 3.0 µs on the A-scan. Determine (i) the wavelength of the ultrasonic wave, (ii) the depth of the flaw below the entry surface. *(7 marks)*

### Model Solution for Q3(A)

#### Part (a) — NDT Classification (7 marks)

**Surface-Open Methods** *(2 marks)*
1. **Visual Testing (VT)** — optical observation; principle = direct/indirect visual acuity (naked eye, borescope, fiberscope).
2. **Dye Penetrant Testing (DPT)** — capillary action; principle = low-surface-tension liquid is drawn into a surface-breaking crack by capillarity, then drawn back out by a porous developer.

**Volumetric / Subsurface Methods** *(2 marks)*
3. **Ultrasonic Testing (UT)** — elastic wave propagation; principle = high-frequency sound waves reflect from acoustic-impedance mismatches ($Z = \rho c$).
4. **Radiographic Testing (RT)** — ionising radiation absorption; principle = differential X-ray/γ-ray attenuation (Beer–Lambert law) reveals internal density variations.

**Dynamic / In-Service Methods** *(2 marks)*
5. **Acoustic Emission Testing (AET)** — transient stress-wave release; principle = sudden energy release (e.g., crack jump, fibre break) emits elastic waves that are detected by sensitive piezo sensors.
6. **Infrared Thermography (IRT)** — black-body radiation; principle = temperature anomalies (delamination, fluid leakage, impact damage) are detected as surface-emitted infrared radiation contrasts.

*(Closing sentence: 1 mark)* — *"NDT selection is governed by the suspected defect type: surface, subsurface, or in-service dynamic."*

#### Part (b) — UT Numerical (7 marks)

**Given:** $f = 4$ MHz $= 4 \times 10^6$ Hz; $v = 5900$ m/s; plate thickness $L = 10$ mm; flaw echo time $t = 3.0$ µs $= 3.0 \times 10^{-6}$ s.

**Step (i) — Wavelength** *(3 marks)*

$$
\lambda = \frac{v}{f} = \frac{5900}{4 \times 10^6}
$$

$$
\boxed{\lambda = 1.475 \times 10^{-3}\;\text{m} = 1.475\;\text{mm}}
$$

**Step (ii) — Flaw depth** *(4 marks)*

The pulse travels down to the flaw and back, so total path = $2d$.

$$
t = \frac{2d}{v} \quad\Longrightarrow\quad d = \frac{vt}{2}
$$

$$
d = \frac{5900 \times 3.0 \times 10^{-6}}{2}
$$

$$
\boxed{d = 8.85 \times 10^{-3}\;\text{m} = 8.85\;\text{mm}}
$$

**Sanity check** *(1 mark)*: The flaw depth is 8.85 mm, which is less than the plate thickness of 10 mm. The back-wall echo would have arrived at $t_{bw} = 2L/v = 2(0.01)/5900 = 3.39\;\mu s$, which is *later* than the observed 3.0 µs echo — consistent with a flaw located *before* the back wall. ✓

---

> **Q3(B). [KTU University Exam – Model Paper, alternate choice]** *(14 marks)*  
> *(a)* Explain the principle of Magnetic Particle Testing (MPT). Why is it restricted to ferromagnetic materials? *(7 marks)*  
> *(b)* An aluminium plate ($\sigma = 3.5 \times 10^7$ S/m, $\mu = \mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}$ H/m) is inspected by Eddy Current Testing at 200 kHz. Compute the skin depth. If the same plate is inspected at 800 kHz, by what factor does the skin depth change? *(7 marks)*

### Model Solution for Q3(B)

#### Part (a) — MPT Principle (7 marks)

**Principle (4 marks):** A ferromagnetic specimen is magnetised, either by a permanent magnet, an electromagnet (yoke), or by passing a high current through the part itself. Under normal conditions, the **magnetic flux lines** are uniform and confined within the material. At a **surface-breaking or near-surface defect** (crack, laps, cold shut, porosity), the magnetic circuit is interrupted. The flux lines are forced to **leak out of the surface** and re-enter a short distance away, forming a localised **leakage field**. When fine ferromagnetic particles (dry black powder, or wet fluorescent magnetic particles suspended in a carrier liquid) are applied to the surface, they are **attracted to the leakage field** and accumulate at the defect site, making it visible to the inspector as a sharp "magnetic indication."

**Restriction to ferromagnetic materials (3 marks):** MPT depends on the ability of the material to be **magnetised to a high flux density** ($\mu_r \gg 1$). In ferromagnetic materials (iron, nickel, cobalt, most steels), the magnetic domains align under the applied field, producing strong flux and detectable leakage. In **paramagnetic** materials (austenitic stainless steel, aluminium, copper) the relative permeability is $\approx 1$ and there is no significant flux concentration; the leakage field at a defect is orders of magnitude smaller and cannot attract particles. Therefore MPT is fundamentally restricted to **ferromagnetic** parts — this is a *physics* limitation, not a procedural one.

#### Part (b) — ET Skin-Depth Numerical (7 marks)

**Given:** $\sigma = 3.5 \times 10^7$ S/m; $\mu = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}$ H/m; $f_1 = 200$ kHz; $f_2 = 800$ kHz.

**Step 1 — Recall the skin-depth formula.** *(1 mark)*

$$
\delta = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\pi f \mu \sigma}}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute $\delta$ at 200 kHz.** *(3 marks)*

$$
\delta_1 = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\pi \times 2\times 10^5 \times 4\pi \times 10^{-7} \times 3.5\times 10^7}}
$$

Evaluate the product inside the square root:

$$
\pi \times 2\times 10^5 \times 4\pi \times 10^{-7} \times 3.5\times 10^7 = 4\pi^2 \times 0.7 \times 10^{6}
$$

Numerically, $4\pi^2 \approx 39.48$, so the product $\approx 27.6 \times 10^6$. The square root is $\sqrt{27.6 \times 10^6} \approx 5254$.

$$
\boxed{\delta_1 \approx \frac{1}{5254} \approx 1.90 \times 10^{-4}\;\text{m} = 0.190\;\text{mm}}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute $\delta$ at 800 kHz using the $\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{f}}$ scaling.** *(3 marks)*

Since $\delta \propto 1/\sqrt{f}$,

$$
\frac{\delta_2}{\delta_1} = \sqrt{\frac{f_1}{f_2}} = \sqrt{\frac{200}{800}} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{4}} = \frac{1}{2}
$$

$$
\delta_2 = \frac{\delta_1}{2} = \frac{0.190}{2} = 0.095\;\text{mm}
$$

**Factor change = ½ (skin depth reduced by 50%).** *(1 mark for stating the qualitative relationship)*

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit conversion errors** — leaving $f$ in kHz without converting to Hz in the formula is the #1 numerical mistake. Always write $f = 200 \times 10^3$ Hz inside the square root.
> 2. **Confusing $\mu$ with $\mu_r$** — the formula uses **absolute permeability** $\mu = \mu_0 \mu_r$. For aluminium, $\mu_r \approx 1$, so $\mu \approx \mu_0$. For steel, $\mu_r$ can be 100–1000, and the skin depth collapses dramatically (this is why ET is unsuitable for ferromagnetic parts in absolute terms).
> 3. **Writing $v = f\lambda$ with no derivation** — for the Part-B UT question, you must derive the relation from the wave equation (or at least state it from the dispersion relation). Plugging numbers into an unstated formula costs you the first mark.
> 4. **Forgetting the factor of 2 in pulse-echo time-of-flight** — students often write $d = vt$ instead of $d = vt/2$. This is an automatic 1-mark loss in KTU 2024 valuation.
> 5. **Naming an NDT method without justifying the choice** — always answer "Why this method?" in one sentence (e.g., "DPT is chosen because the suspected crack is surface-breaking and the part is non-magnetic austenitic stainless steel").

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist** — read this section twice in the last hour before the exam.

- [x] **Definition:** NDT evaluates a component *without impairing its future usefulness*. It is the **first** tier of the forensic tool box, invoked *before* any destructive sampling.
- [x] **Failure Mechanism vs. Mode:** *Mechanism* is the underlying process (fatigue, creep, SCC). *Mode* is the observable outcome (rupture, deformation, leakage). Always separate the two.
- [x] **Six Core NDT Methods:** VT, DPT, MPT, RT, UT, ET, plus dynamic methods (AET, IRT).
- [x] **VT is always first** — cheapest, fastest, and reveals ~70 % of defects in well-lit conditions.
- [x] **DPT works only on surface-breaking defects** in non-porous materials. It cannot find subsurface flaws.
- [x] **MPT is restricted to ferromagnetic materials** (high $\mu_r$). It detects surface and near-surface defects.
- [x] **RT requires radiation safety protocol.** Beer–Lambert: $I = I_0 e^{-\mu x}$. Dense regions appear light on film; voids appear dark.
- [x] **UT fundamentals:** $v = f\lambda$ (derived from 1-D wave equation), pulse-echo depth $d = vt/2$, Snell's law $\sin\theta_1/\sin\theta_2 = v_1/v_2$, reflection coefficient $R = (Z_2 - Z_1)/(Z_2 + Z_1)$.
- [x] **ET skin depth:** $\delta = 1/\sqrt{\pi f \mu \sigma}$; doubles $f \Rightarrow$ halves $\delta$.
- [x] **Acoustic impedance mismatch** ($Z = \rho c$) is the *cause* of all UT echoes. Crack-filled-with-air $\approx$ total reflector.
- [x] **Fracture-mechanics link:** A measured NDT crack size $a_i$ feeds the **Paris law** $da/dN = C(\Delta K)^m$ to predict remaining life $N_f$. For $m = 3$, $N_f = 2(a_i^{-1/2} - a_c^{-1/2})/[C(Y\Delta\sigma\sqrt{\pi})^3]$.
- [x] **KTU favourite sequence:** For a failed nozzle-weld on a pressure vessel — **VT → DPT → UT/RT → metallography → chemistry → conclusion**.
- [x] **Conversion traps:** Always convert kHz → Hz, µs → s, mm → m *before* substituting into any NDT formula.
- [x] **"Why this method?" rule:** Every NDT choice in a 14-mark answer must be justified with (a) defect type, (b) material class, and (c) accessibility.
- [x] **Iterative workflow:** NDT findings often prompt *re-inspection* at higher resolution. The forensic process is **iterative**, not linear.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Crack Detection and Human Eye - Hardness Testing - Destructive Testing Methods with Case Studies
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECET724 - FORENSIC ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Crack Detection and Human Eye - Hardness Testing - Destructive Testing Methods with Case Studies

# MODULE 3 — CRACK DETECTION, HARDNESS TESTING & DESTRUCTIVE TESTING METHODS

## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is Forensic Crack Detection?

In forensic engineering, **Crack Detection** is the systematic identification, characterization, and documentation of discontinuities, fractures, and flaws in engineering components and structures. The process begins with the simplest and most fundamental tool — the **unaided human eye** (visual inspection) — and escalates to sophisticated Non-Destructive Testing (NDT) and Destructive Testing (DT) methodologies as warranted by the failure investigation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
> *Crack detection in forensic engineering is the application of optical, mechanical, chemical, and physical examination techniques to identify, measure, and characterize the presence, geometry, and origin of cracks in engineering materials and structures, conducted to reconstruct the failure sequence and assign causal responsibility.*

### 1.2 The Human Eye as a Crack Detection Instrument

The human visual system is, surprisingly, the **most frequently used crack detection instrument in forensic engineering worldwide**. Although limited in resolution, the eye is fast, mobile, and capable of pattern recognition that no machine can match in preliminary triage.

**Key Optical Parameters of the Human Eye:**

| Parameter | Value / Range | Forensic Significance |
|---|---|---|
| Visual Acuity (Snellen 20/20) | **1 arc-minute (≈ 0.0003 rad)** | Minimum separable angle |
| Static Acuity | 1.0 mm @ 1 m distance | Minimum resolvable crack length |
| Practical crack detection limit (unaided) | **0.1 – 0.2 mm wide** | Under good illumination |
| Photopic (cone) vision | 1.0 lux to 10⁶ lux | Daylight inspection range |
| Color discrimination (Δλ) | ≈ 1 – 3 nm | Identifying oxidation tints |
| Accommodation range | 25 cm to ∞ | Variable working distance |
| Depth perception (stereopsis) | 10 – 20 arc-seconds | Useful for surface relief cracks |

> [!NOTE]
> **Resolution Limit of the Unaided Eye:** The smallest crack width reliably detectable by the unaided human eye under standard forensic inspection lighting (≥ 500 lux, white light, magnification 1×) is approximately **0.1 mm (100 μm)**. Cracks below this threshold require magnification (10× hand lens, stereo microscope) or NDT methods.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Detective's Magnifying Glass

Imagine you are a detective at a crime scene. Before pulling out sophisticated forensic kits, you first **look** at the scene. You notice a broken window, a smeared fingerprint, a tear in fabric. The same applies to a broken shaft or a collapsed beam — the forensic engineer first **walks around, observes, and visually maps** the failure. The human eye provides:

1. **Holistic scene understanding** — the global picture of failure.
2. **Pattern recognition** — fatigue striations, hackle marks, chevron patterns.
3. **Color cues** — oxidation tints, oil staining, paint cracks.
4. **Anomaly detection** — the "this looks wrong" instinct.

It is only after this visual triage that more expensive methods (microscopy, hardness testing, fractography, chemical analysis) are deployed.

### 1.4 Hardness Testing in Forensic Engineering

**Hardness Testing** is a quasi-destructive or destructive mechanical test in which a standardized indenter is forced into the surface of a material under a known load, and the size or depth of the permanent indentation is measured. The resulting hardness number correlates with the material's **strength, wear resistance, and microstructural condition**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Forensic Utility of Hardness Testing:**
> 1. **Material Identification** — distinguishing steel grades (e.g., 1018 vs 4140).
> 2. **Heat Treatment Verification** — confirming whether a component was quenched/tempered.
> 3. **Weld Integrity Assessment** — detecting HAZ softening.
> 4. **Counterfeit Detection** — verifying claimed alloy specifications.
> 5. **Carbon Content Estimation** — for steels, $HRC \approx f(\%C)$.
> 6. **Work Hardening / Strain Evidence** — local hardness rise near a fracture origin.

### 1.5 Destructive Testing (DT) Methods — Definition

**Destructive Testing** involves deliberately loading, sectioning, etching, or chemically treating a specimen to failure or to a state that reveals internal information that cannot be obtained non-destructively. Unlike NDT, DT **consumes the evidence** — it is therefore conducted only after NDT and visual documentation are complete.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Destructive Methods in Forensic Engineering:**
> - **Tensile Testing** (yield strength, UTS, % elongation, reduction in area)
> - **Impact Testing** (Charpy V-notch, Izod)
> - **Hardness Testing** (Brinell, Vickers, Rockwell, Knoop, Microhardness)
> - **Fractographic Sectioning** (mounting, polishing, etching)
> - **Macroscopic & Microscopic Examination** (grain size, inclusion rating, weld profile)
> - **Chemical Spot Tests & Wet Analysis**
> - **Bend / Flattening Tests**
> - **Welded Joint Sectioning (transverse/longitudinal)**

### 1.6 Intuitive Overview of the Investigative Flow

> [!NOTE]
> **The Forensics Ladder of Evidence (Conceptual):**
> **Level 1** — Human Eye (Visual) → **Level 2** — Hand Lens (10×) → **Level 3** — NDT (DPT, MPI, UT, RT, ET) → **Level 4** — Hardness Testing (semi-destructive) → **Level 5** — Macro Sectioning → **Level 6** — Microstructural Analysis → **Level 7** — Mechanical DT (Tensile/Impact).
>
> Each higher level **consumes more of the evidence** but **reveals more truth**.

---

## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Visual Crack Detection — Physical Principles

The detectability of a crack by the human eye is governed by four physics-based parameters:

1. **Contrast** — $\Delta L = \vert L_{crack} - L_{surface} \vert$, where $L$ is luminance. Detection threshold typically $\Delta L \geq 0.1$ to $0.2$.
2. **Spatial Frequency Response (MTF)** — the eye's Modulation Transfer Function peaks at **3 – 5 cycles/degree**, dropping to zero beyond **30 cycles/degree**.
3. **Illumination Angle** — oblique lighting (15°–30° from surface) maximizes shadow contrast for surface-breaking cracks.
4. **Crack Geometry** — width $w$, depth $d$, length $\ell$. A crack with $w < 0.05$ mm is invisible to the naked eye.

**Rayleigh's Criterion for the Human Eye:**

$$\theta_{min} = 1.22 \frac{\lambda}{D_{pupil}}$$

With $\lambda \approx 550$ nm and $D_{pupil} \approx 2$ mm (daylight):

$$\theta_{min} \approx 1.22 \times \frac{550 \times 10^{-9}}{2 \times 10^{-3}} \approx 3.36 \times 10^{-4} \text{ rad}$$

At a working distance of 250 mm (near point of vision):

$$x_{min} = \theta_{min} \times D_{near} \approx 0.084 \text{ mm} \approx 84\ \mu m$$

This **theoretical lower limit (~ 84 μm)** is consistent with the empirical forensic detection limit of **~ 100 μm**.

### 2.2 Visual Inspection Standards (Forensic Relevance)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU/Industry Reference Standards for Visual Inspection:**
> - **ASTM E2707** – Standard Practice for Contact Visual Inspection
> - **ASTM E2862** – Standard Practice for Visual Inspection of As-Built Manufactured Products
> - **ASME BPVC Section V, Article 9** – Visual Examination
> - **AWS QC1** – Qualification of Visual Welding Inspectors
> - **ISO 9712** – Qualification of NDT Personnel
> - **BS EN 13018** – Visual Testing (VT) General Principles

### 2.3 Brinell Hardness Test — Theory

In the **Brinell Hardness Test (BHN)**, a hardened steel (or tungsten carbide) ball of diameter $D$ is pressed into the specimen with a load $F$ for a fixed dwell time. The diameter $d$ of the residual impression is measured.

**Derivation of BHN Formula:**

The Brinell Hardness Number is defined as the applied load divided by the curved surface area of the indentation:

$$BHN = \frac{F}{A_{spherical\ cap}}$$

The spherical cap area (lune of the indentation) is:

$$A = \pi D h$$

where $h$ is the depth of the indentation. From geometry, with impression diameter $d$:

$$h = \frac{D}{2} - \frac{\sqrt{D^2 - d^2}}{2}$$

Substituting:

$$A = \pi D \left( \frac{D - \sqrt{D^2 - d^2}}{2} \right)$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{BHN = \frac{2F}{\pi D \left( D - \sqrt{D^2 - d^2} \right)}}$$

**Standardized test conditions (ASTM E10):**

| Material | Ball $D$ (mm) | Load $F$ (kgf) | $F/D^2$ ratio |
|---|---|---|---|
| Steel / Cast Iron | 10 | 3000 | 30 |
| Copper / Brass | 10 | 1000 | 10 |
| Aluminum | 10 | 500 | 5 |
| Lead / Tin | 10 | 250 | 2.5 |
| Bearings | 10 | 1000 | 10 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Forensic Note:** Brinell leaves a **large, visible impression** (2–6 mm diameter) and is therefore **NOT suitable for finished/forensic evidence surfaces**. It is used on **coupons or witness samples** taken from the failure location.

### 2.4 Vickers Hardness Test — Theory

The **Vickers Test (HV)** uses a **square-based pyramidal diamond indenter** with a face angle of $\alpha = 136°$. The two diagonals $d_1$ and $d_2$ of the indentation are measured, and the mean $d = (d_1 + d_2)/2$ is used.

**Derivation of HV Formula:**

The indenter forms a square impression with diagonal $d$. The slant height of the pyramid is $d / (2 \sin(\alpha/2)) = d / (2 \sin 68°)$. The base area of the pyramid is:

$$A = \frac{d^2}{2 \sin(\alpha/2) \cos(\alpha/2)} \cdot \sin(\alpha/2) = \frac{d^2}{2 \sin(\alpha/2) \cos(\alpha/2)} \cdot \text{adjustment}$$

For $\alpha = 136°$:

$$A = \frac{d^2}{2 \sin(68°)} = \frac{d^2}{1.854}$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{HV = \frac{1.854 \cdot F}{d^2} = 0.1891 \frac{F}{d^2}}$$

with $F$ in **N** and $d$ in **mm**, yielding $HV$ dimensionless. If $F$ is in **kgf**, then $HV = 0.1891 \cdot F / d^2$ still applies with the standard constant.

> [!NOTE]
> **Forensic Advantage of Vickers:** Can be used with **loads as low as 10 gf (microhardness)** — ideal for **thin foils, weld HAZ profiling, case-hardened layers, and archaeological forensic artifacts**.

### 2.5 Rockwell Hardness Test — Theory

The **Rockwell Test (HR)** measures the **depth of plastic penetration** rather than indentation area. A diamond cone (HRA, HRC) or steel ball (HRB, HRF, etc.) is used. The procedure is:

1. Apply a **minor load** $F_0 = 10$ kgf → establish reference depth $h_0$.
2. Apply **major load** $F_1$ (60, 100, or 150 kgf) → penetration $h_1$.
3. Remove $F_1$, retain $F_0$ → elastic recovery gives final depth $h$.
4. Define:

$$\boxed{HR = 100 - \frac{h}{0.002} \quad \text{(Diamond Cone, e.g., HRC)}}$$

$$\boxed{HR = 130 - \frac{h}{0.002} \quad \text{(Steel Ball, e.g., HRB)}}$$

where $h$ is in mm.

**Common Rockwell Scales:**

| Scale | Indenter | Load (kgf) | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| HRA | Diamond Cone | 60 | Carbides, thin steel, shallow HRC |
| HRB | 1/16" ball | 100 | Soft steel, Cu alloys, Al alloys |
| HRC | Diamond Cone | 150 | Hardened steels, tool steels |
| HRD | Diamond Cone | 100 | Thin steel, case-hardened surfaces |
| HRF | 1/16" ball | 60 | Soft Al, soft Cu |
| HRG | 1/16" ball | 150 | Phosphor bronze, gun metal |
| HRN/T (Superficial) | Diamond/ball | 15/30/45 | Plated layers, thin sections |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Forensic Note:** Rockwell C leaves a small, measurable impression (~ 0.3 mm deep). It is **acceptable for in-service forensic examination** of broken shafts, gears, and components. The test is **fast (~ 5 s)**, requires minimal surface preparation, and is **standardized in ASTM E18**.

### 2.6 Knoop Hardness Test — Theory

The **Knoop Test (HK)** uses an elongated rhombic-based pyramidal diamond indenter with included longitudinal angle 172.5° and transverse angle 130°. The long diagonal $L$ is measured.

**Derivation of HK Formula:**

The projected area of the Knoop impression is:

$$A_p = \frac{L^2}{2 \cot(\theta_1/2) \tan(\theta_2/2)}$$

where $\theta_1 = 172.5°$ and $\theta_2 = 130°$. Substituting the angles:

$$A_p = \frac{L^2}{2 \cot(86.25°) \tan(65°)} = \frac{L^2}{2 \cdot (0.0633) \cdot (2.1445)} \approx \frac{L^2}{0.2714}$$

Therefore (in kgf/mm²):

$$\boxed{HK = \frac{F}{A_p} = \frac{14.229 \cdot F \text{(kgf)}}{L^2 \text{(mm}^2\text{)}}}$$

In SI units ($F$ in N):

$$HK = \frac{0.01423 \cdot F \text{(N)}}{L^2 \text{(mm}^2\text{)}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Forensic Application:** Knoop is the **gold standard for forensic microhardness mapping** — revealing **work-hardened zones near fatigue origins, decarburization at fracture surfaces, weld HAZ softening, and case-depth profiles of carburized/nitrided parts**.

### 2.7 Hardness–Strength Correlation (Forensic Power)

Hardness testing becomes a powerful forensic instrument when converted to strength estimates:

**For Steels (Empirical, valid for $\sigma_{uts} \leq 1400$ MPa):**

$$\boxed{\sigma_{uts} \text{(MPa)} \approx 3.45 \times HBN}$$

**For Steels (HRC, valid for HRC 20–60):**

$$\sigma_{uts} \text{(MPa)} \approx 980 + 805 \cdot (HRC - 20) \text{ (approximate, varies with alloy)}$$

**Hardness to approximate conversion (for forensic use):**

| HB | HV | HRC | Approx. $\sigma_{uts}$ (MPa) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 200 | 200 | 11 | 690 |
| 300 | 305 | 30 | 1035 |
| 400 | 415 | 43 | 1380 |
| 500 | 525 | 52 | 1725 |
| 600 | 640 | 58 | 2070 |

### 2.8 Charpy Impact Test — Theory

The **Charpy V-Notch Test (ASTM E23)** measures the energy absorbed during fracture of a standardized notched specimen (10×10×55 mm, 45° V-notch 2 mm deep, 0.25 mm root radius) struck by a swinging pendulum.

$$\boxed{E_{abs} = m g L (\cos\beta - \cos\alpha)}$$

where:
- $m$ = pendulum mass
- $L$ = pendulum length
- $\alpha$ = initial lift angle
- $\beta$ = final rise angle after fracture
- $g$ = gravity

Energy is reported in **Joules (J)** with $CVN$ subscript (e.g., 27 J @ −40 °C).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Forensic Note:** Charpy testing **reconstructs brittle-to-ductile transition behavior**. Many catastrophic failures (e.g., Liberty Ships in WWII, Northridge brittle fractures) occurred because actual service temperatures were below the **Ductile-to-Brittle Transition Temperature (DBTT)**. Charpy absorbed energy vs temperature curves are essential forensic evidence.

### 2.9 Tensile Test — Forensic Quantities

$$\sigma = \frac{F}{A_0}, \quad \epsilon = \frac{\Delta L}{L_0}, \quad \%\ El = \frac{L_f - L_0}{L_0} \times 100\%$$

$$\%RA = \frac{A_0 - A_f}{A_0} \times 100\%$$

**True stress–true strain:**

$$\sigma_T = \sigma(1+\epsilon), \quad \epsilon_T = \ln(1+\epsilon)$$

**Strain-hardening law (Hollomon):**

$$\sigma_T = K \epsilon_T^n$$

**Forensic use:** Determines whether material met specified $S_y$, $S_u$, % elongation, and reduction in area — directly falsifies or validates material certification claims.

### 2.10 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-YIELD KTU FORMULA SHEET — CRACK DETECTION & DESTRUCTIVE TESTING**

| Test | Formula | Units | Constants |
|---|---|---|---|
| Visual limit (eye) | $x_{min} = 1.22 \lambda D_{near} / D_{pupil}$ | m | $\lambda = 550$ nm |
| Brinell | $BHN = 2F / [\pi D (D - \sqrt{D^2 - d^2})]$ | kgf/mm² | $D = 10$ mm standard |
| Vickers | $HV = 1.854 F / d^2$ | kgf/mm² | $\alpha = 136°$ |
| Rockwell C | $HRC = 100 - h/0.002$ | unitless | $F_1 = 150$ kgf |
| Rockwell B | $HRB = 130 - h/0.002$ | unitless | $F_1 = 100$ kgf |
| Knoop | $HK = 14.229 F / L^2$ | kgf/mm² | Angles 172.5°/130° |
| Steel $\sigma_u$ | $\sigma_u \approx 3.45 \cdot BHN$ | MPa | Valid ≤ 1400 MPa |
| Charpy | $E = mgL(\cos\beta - \cos\alpha)$ | J | $L$ = pendulum length |
| Tensile stress | $\sigma = F / A_0$ | MPa | $A_0$ = original area |
| Hollomon | $\sigma_T = K \epsilon_T^n$ | MPa | $n$ = 0.0–0.5 |

---

## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Detailed Derivation — Brinell Hardness from Spherical Cap Geometry

We start with the geometric definition of the indentation: a spherical cap of height $h$ removed from a sphere of radius $R = D/2$. The base diameter of the cap is $d$.

**Step 1 — Identify the geometric configuration:**

The indenter is a sphere of diameter $D$ and radius $R = D/2$. The impression is a spherical cap with chord length $d$ and height $h$. From the geometry of a circle:

$$(R - h)^2 + (d/2)^2 = R^2$$

**Step 2 — Expand and simplify:**

$$R^2 - 2Rh + h^2 + \frac{d^2}{4} = R^2$$

Cancel $R^2$:

$$-2Rh + h^2 + \frac{d^2}{4} = 0$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $h$ in terms of $d$:**

Treating $h$ as the unknown:

$$h^2 - 2Rh + \frac{d^2}{4} = 0$$

Using the quadratic formula:

$$h = \frac{2R \pm \sqrt{4R^2 - d^2}}{2} = R \pm \frac{\sqrt{4R^2 - d^2}}{2}$$

Since $h < R$ (shallow cap), take the minus sign:

$$h = R - \frac{\sqrt{4R^2 - d^2}}{2} = \frac{D}{2} - \frac{\sqrt{D^2 - d^2}}{2}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the curved (lune) surface area of the cap:**

The lateral surface area of a spherical cap of height $h$ on a sphere of radius $R$ is:

$$A = 2 \pi R h$$

Substituting $R = D/2$:

$$A = 2 \pi \cdot \frac{D}{2} \cdot h = \pi D h = \pi D \left( \frac{D - \sqrt{D^2 - d^2}}{2} \right)$$

**Step 5 — Define BHN as load divided by this area:**

$$BHN = \frac{F}{A} = \frac{F}{\pi D (D - \sqrt{D^2 - d^2}) / 2} = \frac{2F}{\pi D \left( D - \sqrt{D^2 - d^2} \right)}$$

**Step 6 — Numerical Example:**

Suppose $F = 3000$ kgf, $D = 10$ mm, $d = 4.5$ mm.

First compute the term under the square root:

$$D^2 - d^2 = 100 - 20.25 = 79.75 \text{ mm}^2$$

$$\sqrt{79.75} = 8.9306 \text{ mm}$$

Now compute the denominator:

$$\pi D (D - \sqrt{D^2 - d^2}) = \pi \times 10 \times (10 - 8.9306) = \pi \times 10 \times 1.0694$$

$$= 33.595 \text{ mm}^2$$

Therefore:

$$BHN = \frac{2 \times 3000}{33.595} = \frac{6000}{33.595} = 178.6 \approx 179 \text{ kgf/mm}^2$$

For a steel with $BHN \approx 179$, we estimate:

$$\sigma_u \approx 3.45 \times 179 = 617.6 \text{ MPa} \approx 618 \text{ MPa}$$

This is consistent with a **medium-carbon steel in the as-rolled or normalized condition**.

### 3.2 Detailed Derivation — Vickers Hardness from Pyramid Geometry

The Vickers indenter is a square-base pyramid with face-to-opposite-face angle of $\alpha = 136°$. The face-to-axis angle is therefore $\alpha/2 = 68°$.

**Step 1 — Diagonal-to-depth relationship:**

The face of the pyramid makes an angle of $68°$ with the base plane. The diagonal $d$ of the square impression and the depth $h_p$ of the pyramid are related by:

$$h_p = \frac{d}{2 \tan(68°)} = \frac{d}{2 \times 2.4751} = \frac{d}{4.9502}$$

**Step 2 — Slant height of pyramid face:**

The slant height of each triangular face is:

$$\ell = \frac{d}{2 \sin(68°)} = \frac{d}{2 \times 0.9272} = \frac{d}{1.8544}$$

**Step 3 — Area of one triangular face:**

$$A_{face} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot d \cdot \ell = \frac{1}{2} \cdot d \cdot \frac{d}{1.8544} = \frac{d^2}{3.7088}$$

**Step 4 — Total slant surface area of the four faces:**

$$A_{total} = 4 \times \frac{d^2}{3.7088} = \frac{d^2}{0.9272} = \frac{d^2}{2 \sin(68°)}$$

**Step 5 — Define HV as load over this area:**

$$HV = \frac{F}{A_{total}} = \frac{F \cdot 2 \sin(68°)}{d^2} = \frac{1.8544 \cdot F}{d^2}$$

The constant $1.8544$ is normally rounded to $1.854$. This is the **exact geometric derivation** of the Vickers constant.

**Step 6 — Numerical Example:**

Suppose $F = 30$ kgf, $d_1 = 0.480$ mm, $d_2 = 0.490$ mm.

Mean diagonal:

$$d = \frac{d_1 + d_2}{2} = \frac{0.480 + 0.490}{2} = 0.485 \text{ mm}$$

Square the mean:

$$d^2 = 0.485^2 = 0.235225 \text{ mm}^2$$

Compute HV:

$$HV = \frac{1.854 \times 30}{0.235225} = \frac{55.62}{0.235225} = 236.4 \approx 236 \text{ kgf/mm}^2$$

Convert to approximate HRC using empirical tables: $HV \approx 236 \Rightarrow HRC \approx 21$.

### 3.3 Detailed Derivation — Charpy Impact Energy from Pendulum Mechanics

A Charpy pendulum of mass $m$ is raised to an initial angle $\alpha$ from vertical, then released. It swings down, strikes the specimen, and rises to a final angle $\beta$ on the other side.

**Step 1 — Initial potential energy:**

The center of mass of the pendulum is at a height $h_i$ above its lowest point:

$$h_i = L - L\cos\alpha = L(1 - \cos\alpha)$$

Initial PE:

$$PE_i = m g h_i = mgL(1 - \cos\alpha)$$

**Step 2 — Final potential energy after fracture:**

$$h_f = L(1 - \cos\beta)$$

Final PE:

$$PE_f = mgL(1 - \cos\beta)$$

**Step 3 — Energy absorbed (Kinetic energy at impact − kinetic energy after):**

By energy conservation:

$$E_{abs} = PE_i - PE_f = mgL[(1 - \cos\alpha) - (1 - \cos\beta)]$$

$$\boxed{E_{abs} = mgL(\cos\beta - \cos\alpha)}$$

**Step 4 — Numerical Example:**

Suppose $m = 21.6$ kg, $L = 0.80$ m, $\alpha = 160°$, $\beta = 95°$.

Convert to radians for safety (or keep degrees — calculator handles both):

$$\cos(160°) = -0.9397$$

$$\cos(95°) = -0.0872$$

Compute:

$$E_{abs} = 21.6 \times 9.81 \times 0.80 \times [(-0.0872) - (-0.9397)]$$

$$= 169.5 \times [0.8525]$$

$$= 144.5 \text{ J}$$

This represents a **ductile fracture** at the test temperature (e.g., 27 J is the typical minimum for low-carbon steel at room temperature; 144.5 J indicates a tough, ductile material).

### 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — Choosing the Right Hardness Test (Forensic Context)

| Criterion | Brinell | Vickers | Rockwell C | Knoop |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Indenter | 10 mm steel/WC ball | Diamond pyramid | 120° diamond cone | Elongated diamond |
| Load range | 500–3000 kgf | 1–120 kgf | 150 kgf | 10 g – 1 kgf |
| Imprint size | Large (2–6 mm) | Small (0.1–1 mm) | Small (0.3 mm) | Very small (~ 50 μm) |
| Best for | Bulk forgings, castings | Lab/forensic standard | Hardened steels | Microstructure, HAZ |
| Surface prep needed | Low | Moderate (polish) | Moderate | High (polish + etch) |
| Cyclic repeatability | ± 3% | ± 2% | ± 0.5% | ± 1% |
| Portability | Bench only | Bench/lab | Portable available | Lab only |
| Indication of HAZ softening | Poor | Good | Moderate | Excellent |
| Indication of decarburization | Poor | Good | Moderate | Excellent |
| Suitable for finished surfaces | NO | Yes (low load) | Yes (C-scale) | Yes (micro) |

### 3.5 Case Study A — Hyatt Regency Walkway Collapse (1981, Kansas City, USA)

**Background:** Two suspended walkways in the Hyatt Regency Hotel collapsed during a tea dance, killing 114 and injuring 216.

**Failure Mechanism:** A design change doubled the load on the upper walkway's tie rods. The box-beam-to-rod connection detail was changed from a continuous rod passing through both walkways (one nut) to two separate rods with one nut each (the second nut was added in the field). This doubled the load on the box-beam-to-rod nut. The original continuous-rod detail is believed to have been able to carry the load; the field-modified detail failed.

**Forensic Destructive Testing Conducted:**

1. **Tensile testing** of the salvaged tie rods (ASTM A36 steel) showed $\sigma_y = 248$ MPa, $\sigma_u = 410$ MPa — **conforming to specification**.
2. **Charpy V-Notch testing** of the same material at 21 °C showed $CVN = 18$ J — **low toughness**, but not the primary cause.
3. **Hardness testing (HRB and HV)** confirmed material consistency with A36.
4. **Metallographic sectioning** of the failed threaded connection revealed poor thread engagement and **decarburization at the thread root**.
5. **The key forensic conclusion** came not from DT but from **load-path reconstruction** — the destructive tests *exonerated the material* and shifted blame to **the design change and field modification**.

**Lesson:** Destructive testing here proved that **the material was innocent** — a critical forensic step that prevents misdirected blame.

### 3.6 Case Study B — Aloha Airlines Flight 243 (1988)

**Background:** A Boeing 737 lost an 18-foot section of its upper fuselage skin in flight at 24,000 ft over Hawaii. One flight attendant died.

**Forensic Investigation:**

1. **Visual inspection** revealed multiple fatigue cracks and extensive **multiple-site damage (MSD)** along riveted lap joints.
2. **Hardness testing (Vickers microhardness)** on cross-sections of the 2024-T3 aluminum skin showed hardness values consistent with the **T3 temper** — material was correctly heat-treated.
3. **Destructive sectioning** of riveted lap joints revealed **widespread fatigue cracking in the inner skin** (not visible from outside — the failure went undetected by visual inspection alone, even though post-failure visual checks would have shown it).
4. **Tensile testing** of removed skin coupons showed $\sigma_u = 470$ MPa — within specification for 2024-T3, but the **fatigue performance** had degraded due to MSD.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Lesson:** The case demonstrated the **limitation of the unaided human eye** in detecting multi-site fatigue damage in lap joints. After Flight 243, the FAA mandated **NDI of all Boeing 737 lap joints**, and the industry developed the **lap-joint inspection robots** (Bourgonje / Boeing).

### 3.7 Case Study C — The Liberty Ship Failures (1942–1947)

**Background:** During WWII, 469 of the 2,710 Liberty Ships built suffered **brittle fractures**, with 20 breaking completely in half. Many of these fractures initiated at **square hatch corners** and propagated catastrophically, sometimes in calm seas at air temperatures near 0 °C.

**Forensic Destructive Testing:**

1. **Charpy V-Notch impact tests** of the ship steel (ASTM A7, then a relatively new specification) revealed $CVN$ values of only **6.8 J (5 ft·lb) at 21 °C** — extraordinarily low.
2. **Ductile-to-brittle transition curves** showed the **DBTT** of the steel was around **+30 °C** for some heats.
3. **Hardness testing (BHN ≈ 130)** confirmed the steel was a low-carbon, hot-rolled, rimmed steel with **coarse-grain ferrite + pearlite** microstructure.
4. **Metallographic sectioning** revealed large grain size and elongated MnS inclusions — both contributing to low through-thickness toughness.
5. **Notch-sensitivity tests** confirmed the catastrophic loss of toughness at the square hatch corner, where triaxial stress state concentrated stress.

**Outcome:** New specifications mandated **normalized fine-grain steel**, **radiused hatch corners**, **preheating for welds**, and **Charpy V-Notch test acceptance criteria of 18 J at −10 °C minimum**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Lesson:** The Liberty Ship failures are the **founding case study of modern fracture mechanics**. They demonstrated that a single Charpy V-Notch test, properly performed, could have prevented the entire disaster. Today, the **Drop-Weight NDT Test (Pellini)** and **CTOD (Crack Tip Opening Displacement)** tests trace their lineage directly to this forensic case.

### 3.8 Case Study D — Northridge Earthquake Brittle Fractures (1994)

**Background:** During the 1994 Northridge earthquake, **more than 100 welded steel moment-frame buildings** suffered **brittle fractures at beam-to-column connections** in the welded flange region. The earthquake was moderate (M 6.7), yet the damage was severe.

**Forensic Destructive Testing:**

1. **Charpy V-Notch** testing of recovered weld metal and HAZ material revealed absorbed energies as low as **4–10 J at 20 °C** — far below the AISC requirement of **27 J at 21 °C**.
2. **Hardness traverse (Vickers Knoop microhardness, HK 0.5 kgf)** across the weld HAZ showed **peak hardness of 380–420 HK** in the coarse-grain HAZ — indicating **untempered martensite** (excessive hardness) at the HAZ, which is brittle.
3. **Metallographic etching (Nital 2%)** showed **untempered martensite in the HAZ** and **undercut / lack of fusion / slag inclusions** in the welds.
4. **Tensile tests** showed $\sigma_y$ of 340 MPa (A36 specification met), but the **toughness was deficient**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Lesson:** This case established the modern principle that **hardness testing across the weld HAZ is an essential forensic tool** for brittle-fracture investigations. The "Sacificial Moment Frame" and "Reduced Beam Section" (dog-bone) design changes trace back to forensic hardnesstesting evidence from Northridge.

### 3.9 Case Study E — Failed Crane Hook (Generic Forensic)

A forged steel crane hook fails during lifting. Forensic procedure:

1. **Visual inspection** of the fracture surface: locate the origin, measure final-fast-fracture region.
2. **Hardness survey (HRB, 10 mm ball, 1000 kgf) along the hook body:** map hardness vs position.
3. **Microhardness traverse (HV 0.3 kgf) on a section perpendicular to the fracture:** detect decarburization at the surface, residual forging flow lines.
4. **Charpy V-Notch** of extracted specimens at expected service temperature.
5. **Tensile testing** of extracted round bars.
6. **Chemical analysis (spectroscopy + LECO C/S/N/O)** of the bulk material.
7. **Compare all results** to the specified material (e.g., DIN 17100 St 52 or ASTM A668).

Typical forensic findings: a hook that meets tensile strength but has **low CVN** (e.g., 12 J at −20 °C) may be incorrectly heat-treated or contain phosphorus embrittlement.

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 KTU Part A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

> **Q1.** **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 3]**
> *Define the Brinell Hardness Number (BHN) and state the standard test conditions for steel as per ASTM E10.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The Brinell Hardness Number is the ratio of the applied load $F$ to the curved (lune) surface area of the indentation produced by a hardened steel or tungsten-carbide ball pressed into the specimen surface:

$$BHN = \frac{2F}{\pi D \left( D - \sqrt{D^2 - d^2} \right)}$$

For steel (per ASTM E10), the standardized test uses:
- Ball diameter $D = 10$ mm
- Load $F = 3000$ kgf
- $F/D^2$ ratio = 30
- Dwell time: 10–15 s for steel, 30 s for softer metals
- Indentation diameter $d$ measured in two perpendicular directions and averaged.

For non-ferrous materials the test conditions change: for copper alloys $F/D^2 = 10$, for aluminum $F/D^2 = 5$, for lead/tin $F/D^2 = 2.5$.

---

> **Q2.** **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 3]**
> *Explain the role of the unaided human eye as a crack-detection instrument. What is its detection limit, and what physical factor controls it?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The unaided human eye is the **first and most widely used** crack-detection instrument in forensic engineering. It is used in **visual testing (VT)**, providing rapid triage, holistic scene understanding, and pattern recognition of fracture features (chevron marks, beach marks, etc.).

**Detection limit:** Approximately **0.1 mm (100 μm)** crack width under good illumination (≥ 500 lux) and oblique lighting (15–30° from the surface).

**Controlling physical factor:** **Visual acuity**, which is limited by the **diffraction of light through the pupil**. Per Rayleigh's criterion:

$$\theta_{min} = 1.22 \frac{\lambda}{D_{pupil}}$$

With $\lambda = 550$ nm and $D_{pupil} = 2$ mm, $\theta_{min} \approx 3.36 \times 10^{-4}$ rad, giving a minimum resolvable feature of approximately **80–100 μm** at a viewing distance of 250 mm. Below this limit, magnification (hand lens 10×) or NDT methods (DPT, MPI, etc.) are required.

---

### 5.2 KTU Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> **Question A (14 Marks) — Choice 1**
> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 3]**

**(a)** Derive the Brinell hardness formula $BHN = 2F / [\pi D (D - \sqrt{D^2 - d^2})]$ starting from the geometry of a spherical-cap indentation. State clearly the standard test conditions for steel as per ASTM E10. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A failed shaft of unknown grade is subjected to Brinell hardness testing using a 10 mm ball and 3000 kgf load. Two perpendicular impression diameters are measured: $d_1 = 4.20$ mm, $d_2 = 4.30$ mm. Calculate the BHN and estimate the ultimate tensile strength. Comment on whether the material likely meets AISI 1045 medium-carbon steel specification ($\sigma_u = 565$–$725$ MPa). **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

We start with the geometry of the spherical-cap impression. The Brinell ball has diameter $D$ and radius $R = D/2$. The impression is a spherical cap of base diameter $d$ and depth $h$.

**Step 1 — Geometry of a circle of radius $R$:**

The half-chord and the height of the cap are related by the Pythagorean theorem:

$$(R - h)^2 + \left(\frac{d}{2}\right)^2 = R^2$$

**Step 2 — Expand and simplify:**

$$R^2 - 2Rh + h^2 + \frac{d^2}{4} = R^2$$

$$-2Rh + h^2 + \frac{d^2}{4} = 0$$

$$h^2 - 2Rh + \frac{d^2}{4} = 0$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $h$ using the quadratic formula:**

$$h = \frac{2R \pm \sqrt{4R^2 - d^2}}{2} = R \pm \frac{\sqrt{4R^2 - d^2}}{2}$$

Since the cap is shallow, $h < R$, we take the minus sign:

$$h = \frac{D}{2} - \frac{\sqrt{D^2 - d^2}}{2} = \frac{D - \sqrt{D^2 - d^2}}{2}$$

**Step 4 — Spherical cap surface area:**

The lateral (curved) surface area of a spherical cap of height $h$ on a sphere of radius $R$ is:

$$A = 2 \pi R h$$

Substituting $R = D/2$:

$$A = 2 \pi \cdot \frac{D}{2} \cdot \frac{D - \sqrt{D^2 - d^2}}{2} = \frac{\pi D \left( D - \sqrt{D^2 - d^2} \right)}{2}$$

**Step 5 — BHN definition:**

$$BHN = \frac{F}{A} = \frac{2F}{\pi D \left( D - \sqrt{D^2 - d^2} \right)}$$

**Step 6 — ASTM E10 standard conditions for steel:**

- $D = 10$ mm steel (or WC) ball
- $F = 3000$ kgf
- $F/D^2 = 30$
- Dwell time: 10–15 s
- $d$ measured in two perpendicular directions, averaged
- Indentation must satisfy $0.25D \leq d \leq 0.50D$ (i.e., $2.5 \text{ mm} \leq d \leq 5.0 \text{ mm}$ for $D = 10$ mm) for the test to be valid.

---

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

**Step 1 — Mean impression diameter:**

$$d = \frac{d_1 + d_2}{2} = \frac{4.20 + 4.30}{2} = 4.25 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2 — Validate test (must have $2.5 \leq d \leq 5.0$ mm):**

$d = 4.25$ mm — within limits. **Valid test.**

**Step 3 — Compute $d^2$:**

$$d^2 = 4.25^2 = 18.0625 \text{ mm}^2$$

**Step 4 — Compute $D^2 - d^2$:**

$$D^2 - d^2 = 100 - 18.0625 = 81.9375 \text{ mm}^2$$

**Step 5 — Compute $\sqrt{D^2 - d^2}$:**

$$\sqrt{81.9375} = 9.0517 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 6 — Compute $D - \sqrt{D^2 - d^2}$:**

$$D - \sqrt{D^2 - d^2} = 10 - 9.0517 = 0.9483 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 7 — Compute the denominator $\pi D (D - \sqrt{D^2 - d^2})$:**

$$\pi \times 10 \times 0.9483 = 9.483 \pi = 29.793 \text{ mm}^2$$

**Step 8 — Compute BHN:**

$$BHN = \frac{2 \times 3000}{29.793} = \frac{6000}{29.793} = 201.4 \approx 201 \text{ kgf/mm}^2$$

**Step 9 — Estimate ultimate tensile strength:**

$$\sigma_u \approx 3.45 \times BHN = 3.45 \times 201 = 693.5 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 10 — Compare to AISI 1045 spec ($\sigma_u = 565$–$725$ MPa):**

$\sigma_u = 693.5$ MPa lies **within** the AISI 1045 specification. The material **likely meets** AISI 1045 specification for ultimate tensile strength. However, this single test does not verify yield strength, % elongation, or chemistry — additional testing is required for full forensic validation.

---

> **Question B (14 Marks) — Choice 2 (Internal Choice Alternative)**
> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 3]**

**(a)** Describe, with neat sketch, the **Vickers Hardness Test**. Derive the Vickers formula $HV = 1.854 F / d^2$ from the geometry of the square pyramidal indenter (face angle = 136°). **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Two perpendicular diagonals of a Vickers impression are $d_1 = 0.345$ mm, $d_2 = 0.355$ mm, measured with a 30 kgf load. Calculate the Vickers hardness number. A forensic engineer estimates the material's ultimate tensile strength using the empirical rule $\sigma_u = 3.45 \times HV$. Comment on the suitability of this rule and the expected material class. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

**Description (≈ 3 marks):**

The Vickers Hardness Test uses a **square-base pyramidal diamond indenter** with a face angle of $\alpha = 136°$ between opposite faces. A load $F$ (typically 1, 5, 10, 30, 50, or 120 kgf) is applied for 10–15 s. The impression, when viewed under the microscope, is a square. The two diagonals $d_1$ and $d_2$ are measured, and the mean $d = (d_1 + d_2)/2$ is used in the formula. The hardness number is the load divided by the slant surface area of the pyramidal indentation.

The Vickers test can be used for both **macrohardness** (loads ≥ 1 kgf) and **microhardness** (loads 10 gf–1 kgf), making it suitable for thin sections, weld HAZ, and case-hardened layers.

**Derivation (≈ 4 marks):**

The face of the pyramid makes an angle of $68°$ with the base (since $\alpha/2 = 68°$). The slant height of each triangular face is:

$$\ell = \frac{d}{2 \sin(68°)} = \frac{d}{1.8544}$$

The area of one triangular face:

$$A_{face} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot d \cdot \ell = \frac{1}{2} \cdot d \cdot \frac{d}{1.8544} = \frac{d^2}{3.7088}$$

Total slant surface area of the four faces:

$$A_{total} = \frac{4 d^2}{3.7088} = \frac{d^2}{0.9272} = \frac{d^2}{2 \sin(68°)}$$

$$HV = \frac{F}{A_{total}} = \frac{2 F \sin(68°)}{d^2} = \frac{1.8544 F}{d^2} \approx \frac{1.854 F}{d^2}$$

---

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

**Step 1 — Mean diagonal:**

$$d = \frac{d_1 + d_2}{2} = \frac{0.345 + 0.355}{2} = 0.350 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2 — Square of mean:**

$$d^2 = 0.350^2 = 0.1225 \text{ mm}^2$$

**Step 3 — Compute HV:**

$$HV = \frac{1.854 \times 30}{0.1225} = \frac{55.62}{0.1225} = 454.04 \approx 454 \text{ kgf/mm}^2$$

**Step 4 — Compute $\sigma_u$ estimate:**

$$\sigma_u \approx 3.45 \times 454 = 1566.3 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 5 — Material class commentary:**

$HV = 454$ corresponds approximately to **HRC 46** and to a **high-strength, hardened, and tempered alloy steel**, tool steel, or **maraging steel** family. The $\sigma_u$ estimate of ~ 1566 MPa is consistent with materials such as **4340 steel (quenched and tempered)** or a high-alloy tool steel (e.g., H11, H13 in the hardened condition).

**Step 6 — Caveats on the empirical rule $\sigma_u \approx 3.45 \times HV$:**

- Valid for **carbon and low-alloy steels** with $\sigma_u \leq 1400$ MPa.
- For hardened tool steels and maraging steels, the correlation is **approximate** and **tends to overestimate** the actual $\sigma_u$.
- The conversion assumes the same $F/D^2$ ratio between BHN and HV, which is valid only for indentations of similar geometry.
- For **non-ferrous materials** (Al, Cu, Ti), different correlations apply (e.g., $\sigma_u \approx 3.0 \times HV$ for aluminum alloys).
- A direct **tensile test (DT)** is preferred for any forensic investigation where the exact value of $\sigma_u$ is critical.

**Conclusion:** The material is consistent with a **hardened, tempered alloy steel**, but a tensile test and chemical analysis should be conducted to confirm the grade and validate the strength estimate before any forensic attribution of failure cause.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Areas in KTU Module 3 Answers:**
> 1. **Missing units** — KTU examiners *consistently* deduct 0.5–1 mark for missing units in formulas. Always write BHN in kgf/mm², HV in kgf/mm², HRC as dimensionless, $\sigma_u$ in MPa, $E$ in J.
> 2. **Wrong Brinell constant** — Some students write $BHN = F / [\pi D h]$ without deriving $h$ from $d$. The examiner will **only give 5/7 marks** if the derivation is incomplete.
> 3. **Forgetting $F/D^2$ ratio** — When asked for "test conditions for steel," students often write only $F = 3000$ kgf and $D = 10$ mm, but omit the $F/D^2 = 30$ constraint that validates the test.
> 4. **Confusing Vickers face angle (136°) with apex angle (148°)** — These are different. Face angle 136° is between **opposite faces**; apex angle 148° is between **opposite edges**.
> 5. **Mixing HV and HK** — Knoop and Vickers have different indent geometries; the constant 14.229 applies **only to Knoop**, not Vickers.
> 6. **Forgetting to validate Brinell impression diameter** — A test with $d < 2.5$ mm or $d > 5.0$ mm (for $D = 10$ mm) is **invalid** per ASTM E10.
> 7. **Charpy test errors** — Students often forget the **sign convention** in $E = mgL(\cos\beta - \cos\alpha)$: with $\alpha = 160°$ and $\beta = 95°$, the energy absorbed is **positive**, computed as $mgL[(-0.0872) - (-0.9397)] = mgL \times 0.8525$. Mixing up signs gives a **negative** energy and zero marks.
> 8. **In case-study questions**, students often **recite history** but fail to **link destructive testing to the failure mechanism**. Always state: *"The DT showed X, which proves / disproves Y, and therefore the root cause is Z."*

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-DENSITY REVISION CHECKLIST — MODULE 3**

**A. Visual Crack Detection (Human Eye):**
- Unaided eye resolution limit ≈ **0.1 mm** under good lighting
- Rayleigh criterion governs detectability
- Standards: ASTM E2707, ASTM E2862, ASME V Art. 9, ISO 9712, BS EN 13018
- Always use **oblique lighting** (15–30°)
- Always **photograph** before any NDT or DT

**B. Brinell Hardness:**
- $BHN = 2F / [\pi D (D - \sqrt{D^2 - d^2})]$
- Standard for steel: $D = 10$ mm, $F = 3000$ kgf, $F/D^2 = 30$
- Valid impression: $0.25D \leq d \leq 0.50D$
- Forensic limitation: large impression, **NOT** for finished surfaces
- $\sigma_u \approx 3.45 \times BHN$ for steels (≤ 1400 MPa)

**C. Vickers Hardness:**
- $HV = 1.854 F / d^2$ with $F$ in kgf, $d$ in mm
- Face angle 136°; loads 1–120 kgf (macro); 10 gf–1 kgf (micro)
- Suitable for thin sections, weld HAZ, archaeological artifacts
- Microhardness useful for **fracture-origin work-hardening detection**

**D. Rockwell Hardness:**
- $HRC = 100 - h/0.002$ (diamond cone, 150 kgf)
- $HRB = 130 - h/0.002$ (1/16" ball, 100 kgf)
- **Fast (5 s)**, **portable** — ideal for in-field forensics
- Scale selection by material hardness

**E. Knoop Hardness:**
- $HK = 14.229 F / L^2$
- Asymmetric indenter — long diagonal $L$ only is measured
- **Forensic gold standard** for HAZ softening, decarburization, case-depth profiling

**F. Charpy V-Notch:**
- $E = mgL(\cos\beta - \cos\alpha)$
- DBTT (Ductile-to-Brittle Transition Temperature) is a key forensic quantity
- Liberty Ships, Northridge — all forensic investigations hinge on CVN data
- Test in triplicate at multiple temperatures; report mean of three

**G. Tensile Test:**
- $\sigma = F/A_0$, $\epsilon = \Delta L / L_0$, $\%\text{El}$, $\%\text{RA}$
- Validate yield, UTS, % elongation against specifications
- Hollomon: $\sigma_T = K \epsilon_T^n$

**H. Case Studies (must remember):**
1. **Hyatt Regency (1981)** — Design change + thread decarburization; DT exonerated material
2. **Aloha Airlines 243 (1988)** — MSD in lap joints; visual inspection limitation
3. **Liberty Ships (1942–47)** — Low CVN at low temp; Charpy test would have prevented disaster
4. **Northridge EQ (1994)** — Undetected weld HAZ; HK microhardness map revealed untempered martensite
5. **Generic Crane Hook** — Always do hardness survey + Charpy + tensile + chemistry + metallography

**I. Forensic Ladder of Evidence:**
Eye → Hand Lens → NDT → Hardness → Macro Section → Microstructure → Mechanical DT

**J. Universal Rules in Module 3:**
- Always **document photographically** before DT
- Always **section with forensic purpose** — not random samples
- Always **compare results to specifications** — that is the forensic conclusion
- Always **state the assumption** (e.g., $\sigma_u = 3.45 \times BHN$ assumes steel, ≤ 1400 MPa)
- Always **report units** explicitly

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Indirect Stress Strain Analysis - Brittle Lacquer Technique
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECET724 - FORENSIC ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Indirect Stress Strain Analysis - Brittle Lacquer Technique

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Brittle Lacquer Technique (Brittle Coating Method):** A non-destructive, *qualitative* experimental stress analysis technique in which a specially formulated brittle coating (lacquer) is applied to the surface of a structural component. When the component is loaded, the differential strain between the coating and the substrate induces a network of characteristic cracks whose orientation is **perpendicular to the direction of maximum principal tensile strain** ($\varepsilon_1$), thereby revealing the *trajectories* of principal stress on the surface.

The technique is classified as an **Indirect Method** of experimental stress analysis because the investigator does not measure stress or strain directly. Instead, the visual crack pattern serves as a *qualitative indicator*, and quantitative evaluation is performed only when the coating is *calibrated* against a known test beam.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** The brittle lacquer method belongs to the family of **strain-sensitive coatings**. It is purely a *directional* indicator — it tells the analyst **where** high tensile strain exists and **in which direction** it acts, but it does **not** give a numerical magnitude unless coupled with a calibration test.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Eggshell on a Wall"

Imagine you paste a thin, dried eggshell onto the surface of a wall. If the wall is then pulled or bent slightly, the rigid eggshell cannot stretch — it fractures. The cracks that appear will run **at right angles to the direction in which the wall is being stretched most**.

Now replace the eggshell with a **specially engineered brittle lacquer** and the wall with a **machine component**. The lacquer behaves exactly like the eggshell:

- It **bonds** to the substrate.
- It **cannot deform** as much as the metal beneath it.
- When the metal stretches, the lacquer **cracks**.
- The crack lines are **perpendicular to the maximum tensile strain direction**.

The analyst simply *reads* the crack pattern — like reading a contour map of the stress field.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Typical Value / Standard |
|---|---|
| Coating thickness | **0.05 mm – 0.4 mm** |
| Strain sensitivity (threshold strain) | **$\varepsilon_c \approx 300$ to $1500 \, \mu\varepsilon$** |
| Operating temperature | **$20^\circ$C – $40^\circ$C** (most lacquers) |
| Humidity tolerance | **< 70% RH** |
| Crack spacing at saturation | **1 mm – 5 mm** (inversely related to strain) |
| Young's modulus of lacquer | **$\approx 3.5$ GPa** (versus steel $\approx 210$ GPa) |

> [!NOTE]
> The lacquer is roughly **60× less stiff** than steel, but its *failure strain* is **two orders of magnitude lower** than the steel it coats — this stiffness-to-ductility mismatch is what produces the cracks.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operating Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

The method rests on three coupled physical phenomena:

1. **Bonded Composite Action**
   The lacquer is rigidly adhered to the substrate using a compatible primer. Under load, the lacquer *must* follow the surface strain of the component — i.e., $\varepsilon_{\text{lacquer}} = \varepsilon_{\text{substrate, surface}}$.

2. **Strain-Mismatch Fracture**
   Because the lacquer's *critical failure strain* $\varepsilon_c$ is much smaller than the elastic strain limit of the metal, the lacquer fractures first. The fracture criterion is:
   $$\varepsilon_1 \geq \varepsilon_c$$
   where $\varepsilon_1$ is the **maximum principal tensile strain** on the surface.

3. **Crack Geometry Rule**
   Fracture mechanics of brittle thin films dictates that the crack propagates *normal* to the direction of the maximum principal stress (or strain) in a uniaxial tension field. Therefore:
   $$\text{Crack orientation} \perp \sigma_1 \perp \varepsilon_1$$

## 2.2 Why "Indirect"?

The technician never measures a force, a deformation, or a strain gauge output. The information transferred from the structure to the observer is a *visual pattern*. The inference chain is:

$$\text{Visible Cracks} \;\xrightarrow{\text{Geometry}}\; \varepsilon_1\text{-direction} \;\xrightarrow{\text{Boundary conditions}}\; \sigma_1\text{-trajectories}$$

Hence the name *Indirect Stress-Strain Analysis*.

## 2.3 Classification of the Method

| Criterion | Classification |
|---|---|
| Destructiveness | **Non-destructive to the component** (coating is removed with solvent) |
| Quantitative / Qualitative | **Predominantly qualitative**; quantitative only after calibration |
| Type of stress detected | **Tensile principal strain** only — *cannot* detect pure compression directly |
| Directional info | **Yes** — gives principal direction |
| Magnitude info | **Only via calibration** (strain threshold $\varepsilon_c$) |
| Live loading? | **Yes** — cracks form in real time as load is applied |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is the **exam-ready quick reference** for any brittle-lacquer question.

| # | Formula / Relationship | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\varepsilon_1 \geq \varepsilon_c$ | Cracking condition (maximum principal tensile strain must exceed lacquer threshold) | dimensionless ($\mu\varepsilon$) |
| 2 | $\sigma_1 = \dfrac{E}{1-\nu^2}\left(\varepsilon_1 + \nu\,\varepsilon_2\right)$ | Principal stress from principal strain (plane stress) | Pa or MPa |
| 3 | $\sigma_1 - \sigma_2 = \dfrac{E}{1+\nu}\left(\varepsilon_1 - \varepsilon_2\right)$ | Maximum shear stress related form | Pa or MPa |
| 4 | $m = \dfrac{\Delta L}{L}$ | Linear mechanical strain definition | dimensionless |
| 5 | $\varepsilon_c \approx 300$ to $1500\,\mu\varepsilon$ | Lacquer threshold strain (lacquer-specific) | $\mu\varepsilon$ |
| 6 | $S \propto \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{\varepsilon_1 - \varepsilon_c}}$ | Empirical crack spacing vs. strain (calibration form) | mm |
| 7 | $f \propto \varepsilon_1$ | Crack density (cracks per unit length) $\propto$ strain | cracks/mm |
| 8 | $\Delta T_{\text{crit}} = \dfrac{\varepsilon_c}{\alpha_{\text{metal}} - \alpha_{\text{lacquer}}}$ | Maximum allowable temperature variation to avoid spurious cracking | °C |
| 9 | $\varepsilon_{\text{thermal}} = \alpha \cdot \Delta T$ | Thermal strain if temperature changes during test | $\mu\varepsilon$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In LaTeX / KTU exam scripts, write $\sigma_1$ (NOT $\vert\sigma_1\vert$ for absolute value — use $\lvert\sigma_1\rvert$ or $\vert\sigma_1\vert$ *outside* table cells to avoid breaking markdown table syntax).

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Use Case |
|---|---|
| **Aerospace** | Identifying high-stress regions in wing spars, fuselage panels |
| **Pressure vessels** | Mapping stress trajectories around nozzles and fillets |
| **Civil structures** | Locating stress concentrations in bridge girders, crane hooks |
| **Machine design** | Optimising geometry of brackets and castings before final finite-element confirmation |
| **Forensic engineering** | Reconstructing failure conditions by re-creating the loading on a preserved component and studying crack trajectories |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Principal Stress from Principal Strain (Plane Stress)

A surface element of a structural component is in **plane stress** (the third normal stress is zero because the surface is traction-free). Hooke's law for plane stress gives:

$$\varepsilon_x = \frac{1}{E}\left(\sigma_x - \nu\sigma_y\right)$$
$$\varepsilon_y = \frac{1}{E}\left(\sigma_y - \nu\sigma_x\right)$$
$$\gamma_{xy} = \frac{\tau_{xy}}{G} = \frac{2(1+\nu)}{E}\,\tau_{xy}$$

**Step 1 — Invert the equations.** Solve the first two for $\sigma_x$ and $\sigma_y$ in terms of $\varepsilon_x,\varepsilon_y$. Adding the two equations:

$$\varepsilon_x + \varepsilon_y = \frac{1-\nu}{E}\left(\sigma_x + \sigma_y\right)$$

Hence:
$$\sigma_x + \sigma_y = \frac{E}{1-\nu}\left(\varepsilon_x + \varepsilon_y\right)$$

**Step 2 — Strain transformation to principal axes.** The principal strains are eigenvalues of the strain tensor. For plane stress:

$$\varepsilon_{1,2} = \frac{\varepsilon_x + \varepsilon_y}{2} \pm \sqrt{\left(\frac{\varepsilon_x - \varepsilon_y}{2}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{\gamma_{xy}}{2}\right)^2}$$

**Step 3 — Principal stress via Lamé's equations for plane stress.** Combining the inverted Hooke relations:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_1 &= \frac{E}{1-\nu^2}\left(\varepsilon_1 + \nu\,\varepsilon_2\right) \\
\sigma_2 &= \frac{E}{1-\nu^2}\left(\varepsilon_2 + \nu\,\varepsilon_1\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

These are the equations used to convert the *qualitative* crack-direction information from brittle lacquer into a *quantitative* stress estimate **only if strain gauge rosettes** are used alongside for magnitudes.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Direction of Principal Strain from Crack Geometry

Consider a single isolated crack of length $2a$ in a thin brittle film bonded to a substrate. The film is under biaxial strain $(\varepsilon_1,\varepsilon_2)$ with $\varepsilon_1 > \varepsilon_2$ (tensile).

**Step 1 — Griffith energy balance.** The crack will propagate when the release of elastic strain energy per unit crack extension exceeds the surface energy of the new fracture surfaces.

**Step 2 — Mode I dominance.** For a crack in a brittle film under biaxial tension, propagation in pure Mode I requires the crack to open perpendicular to the maximum tensile stress. The energy release rate is maximised when the crack tip lies *normal* to $\sigma_1$.

**Step 3 — Geometric consequence.** Therefore the *crack line* lies in the direction of $\sigma_2$ (or $\varepsilon_2$), i.e. the crack is **perpendicular to the principal tensile direction**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Crack line} \;&\parallel\; \sigma_2 \;\parallel\; \varepsilon_2 \\
\text{Crack normal} \;&\perp\; \sigma_1 \;\perp\; \varepsilon_1
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the *single most important KTU fact* on brittle lacquer — examiners expect it verbatim.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Thermal Spurious Cracking Limit

Suppose the test environment temperature changes by $\Delta T$ between coating application and loading. The lacquer and the substrate expand differently, producing a parasitic thermal strain:

$$\varepsilon_{\text{thermal}} = \left(\alpha_{\text{metal}} - \alpha_{\text{lacquer}}\right)\Delta T$$

To avoid **spurious cracks** (cracks that are not due to load), we must enforce:

$$\varepsilon_{\text{thermal}} < \varepsilon_c$$

Substituting:

$$
\Delta T < \frac{\varepsilon_c}{\alpha_{\text{metal}} - \alpha_{\text{lacquer}}}
$$

For typical numbers: $\varepsilon_c = 600 \times 10^{-6}$, $\alpha_{\text{steel}} = 12 \times 10^{-6}/^\circ\text{C}$, $\alpha_{\text{lacquer}} = 80 \times 10^{-6}/^\circ\text{C}$:

$$
\Delta T < \frac{600 \times 10^{-6}}{(80-12)\times 10^{-6}} = \frac{600}{68} \approx 8.8^\circ\text{C}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is why KTU examiners love asking "why must the test be conducted at a controlled temperature." The answer is the **thermal-strain limit** derived above.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Empirical Crack Spacing Relation (Quantitative Calibration)

When a calibration beam is loaded in pure bending, the lacquer forms a series of *parallel* cracks across the tensile face. As load (and therefore $\varepsilon_1$) increases:

- The first crack appears when $\varepsilon_1 = \varepsilon_c$.
- New cracks appear *between* existing ones as strain continues to rise.
- Empirically, the crack spacing $S$ follows:

$$
S \;\approx\; \frac{k}{\sqrt{\varepsilon_1 - \varepsilon_c}}
$$

where $k$ is a calibration constant (lacquer-specific, $\approx 0.5$ mm·$\sqrt{\mu\varepsilon}$). This is the basis of the **quantitative version** of the technique.

> [!NOTE]
> Crack density $f = 1/S$ is therefore *proportional* to $\sqrt{\varepsilon_1 - \varepsilon_c}$. Use this in calibration charts.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Brittle Lacquer Test Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Component to be Analysed] --> B[Surface Preparation - Clean, Degrease, Light Grit Blast]
    B --> C[Apply Primer Coat - 0.02 mm]
    C --> D[Spray Brittle Lacquer - 0.10 to 0.30 mm Wet, Air Dry 12 to 24 h]
    D --> E[Control Environment - T = 20 to 30 C, RH below 70 percent]
    E --> F[Apply Incremental Static Load - Steps of 10 to 15 percent]
    F --> G{Cracks Visible?}
    G -- No --> F
    G -- Yes --> H[Photograph Crack Pattern at Each Load Step]
    H --> I[Trace Crack Lines on Transparent Overlay]
    I --> J[Identify Principal Strain Trajectories - Perpendicular to Cracks]
    J --> K[Correlate with Strain Gauge Rosette Data]
    K --> L[Compute Principal Stresses via Plane Stress Hookes Law]
    L --> M[Report Stress Map of the Component]

    style A fill:#e6f3ff,stroke:#003366
    style D fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#996600
    style G fill:#f8cecc,stroke:#990000
    style M fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#336600
```

## 4.2 Crack Pattern and Principal Strain Direction (Block Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph surface["Surface Element of Component"]
        dirE1["Direction of epsilon 1 - Maximum Principal Tensile Strain"]
        dirE2["Direction of epsilon 2 - Minimum Principal Strain"]
        crack1["Crack Line - parallel to epsilon 2"]
        crack2["Crack Line - parallel to epsilon 2"]
    end

    dirE1 -. "Perpendicular to crack" .- crack1
    dirE2 -. "Parallel to crack" .- crack1
    dirE1 -. "Perpendicular to crack" .- crack2
    dirE2 -. "Parallel to crack" .- crack2

    style dirE1 fill:#ffe6e6,stroke:#cc0000
    style dirE2 fill:#e6ffe6,stroke:#006600
    style crack1 fill:#ffffff,stroke:#000000
    style crack2 fill:#ffffff,stroke:#000000
```

## 4.3 Test-Setup Block Diagram (Loading, Coating, Observation)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph loading["Loading System"]
        jack["Hydraulic Jack / Dead Weights"]
        loadcell["Load Cell - Calibrated"]
    end

    subgraph component["Test Specimen"]
        specimen["Component with Brittle Lacquer Coating"]
    end

    subgraph observation["Observation Subsystem"]
        light["Directional Light Source - 45 degree"]
        camera["High-Resolution Camera"]
        scale["Reference Scale on Specimen"]
    end

    jack --> loadcell --> specimen
    light -. illuminates .- specimen
    specimen -. reflects crack pattern .- camera
    scale -. included in frame .- camera

    style specimen fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#996600
    style camera fill:#e6f3ff,stroke:#003366
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Fallback Note:** The brittle-lacquer method does not lend itself to a single physical sketch in Mermaid; therefore the **block-level functional architecture** shown above captures the workflow that a KTU examiner would reward.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define the Brittle Lacquer Technique. Why is it classified as an *indirect* method of stress analysis?**

**Model Answer:**
The brittle lacquer technique is a non-destructive experimental stress analysis method in which a brittle coating is applied to a clean metallic surface. When the component is loaded, the lacquer cracks perpendicular to the direction of maximum principal tensile strain $(\varepsilon_1)$, revealing the principal stress trajectories. It is classified as *indirect* because the analyst does not measure stress or strain numerically — the visible crack pattern is the raw observation, and stress magnitude must be inferred via calibration against a known beam or by cross-referencing with strain gauges. *(3 marks)*

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Why must a brittle lacquer test be conducted under controlled temperature and humidity conditions?**

**Model Answer:**
The lacquer is far more sensitive to strain than the metal substrate, with a critical failure strain of only $\varepsilon_c \approx 300\text{–}1500\,\mu\varepsilon$. Any temperature change $\Delta T$ between coating and loading produces a parasitic thermal strain $\varepsilon_{\text{th}} = (\alpha_m - \alpha_L)\Delta T$, which — if it exceeds $\varepsilon_c$ — produces *spurious cracks* unrelated to the applied load. High humidity softens the lacquer and shifts $\varepsilon_c$, distorting calibration. Therefore $\Delta T < \varepsilon_c / (\alpha_m - \alpha_L)$ and RH $< 70\%$ are mandatory. *(3 marks)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A — (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 3]`

**(a)** Explain in detail the **operating principle, procedure, and limitations** of the Brittle Lacquer Technique for stress analysis. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A calibration beam of steel ($E = 210$ GPa, $\nu = 0.3$) is coated with a brittle lacquer of threshold strain $\varepsilon_c = 700\,\mu\varepsilon$. Under a four-point bend test, the strain gauge rosette on the tensile face reads $\varepsilon_x = 1200\,\mu\varepsilon$, $\varepsilon_y = -200\,\mu\varepsilon$, $\gamma_{xy} = 400\,\mu\varepsilon$. Determine the **principal stresses** $\sigma_1$ and $\sigma_2$. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution — (a)

1. **Principle (2 marks):** A brittle lacquer, mechanically bonded to a clean substrate, has a much lower failure strain than the metal. When the structure is loaded, the lacquer cracks wherever the maximum principal tensile strain exceeds the threshold $\varepsilon_c$. The cracks form *perpendicular* to the direction of $\sigma_1 / \varepsilon_1$.

2. **Procedure (3 marks):**
   - *Surface prep*: clean, degrease, lightly abrade.
   - *Primer coat*: 0.02 mm compatible primer.
   - *Lacquer coat*: 0.10–0.30 mm wet film, dried 12–24 h.
   - *Calibration*: a test beam of identical material is loaded to known strain.
   - *Loading*: incremental static load, 10–15% steps.
   - *Observation*: photograph cracks at each step; trace principal trajectories on overlay.
   - *Quantitative conversion*: cross-check with strain rosettes → principal stresses via plane-stress Hooke.

3. **Limitations (2 marks):**
   - Detects only **tensile** principal strain (pure compression invisible).
   - **Qualitative** unless calibration performed.
   - Sensitive to temperature, humidity, vibration.
   - Single-coating thickness gives a narrow strain window.

#### Model Solution — (b)

**Step 1 — Principal strains** (using strain transformation):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\varepsilon_{1,2} &= \frac{\varepsilon_x + \varepsilon_y}{2} \pm \sqrt{\left(\frac{\varepsilon_x - \varepsilon_y}{2}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{\gamma_{xy}}{2}\right)^2} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{1200 + (-200)}{2} \pm \sqrt{\left(\frac{1200 - (-200)}{2}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{400}{2}\right)^2} \\[4pt]
&= 500 \pm \sqrt{(700)^2 + (200)^2} \\[4pt]
&= 500 \pm \sqrt{490000 + 40000} \\[4pt]
&= 500 \pm \sqrt{530000} \\[4pt]
&= 500 \pm 728.0 \;\mu\varepsilon
\end{aligned}
$$

Hence:
$$
\varepsilon_1 = 1228.0\,\mu\varepsilon, \qquad \varepsilon_2 = -228.0\,\mu\varepsilon
$$

**Check**: $\varepsilon_1 = 1228 > \varepsilon_c = 700\,\mu\varepsilon$ — the lacquer *will* crack. ✓

**Step 2 — Principal stresses** (plane stress):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_1 &= \frac{E}{1-\nu^2}\left(\varepsilon_1 + \nu\,\varepsilon_2\right) \\[4pt]
&= \frac{210\,000}{1 - 0.09}\left(1228 \times 10^{-6} + 0.3 \times (-228) \times 10^{-6}\right) \\[4pt]
&= \frac{210\,000}{0.91}\left(1228 - 68.4\right)\times 10^{-6} \\[4pt]
&= 230\,769.2 \times 1159.6 \times 10^{-6} \\[4pt]
&\approx 267.6\;\text{MPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_2 &= \frac{E}{1-\nu^2}\left(\varepsilon_2 + \nu\,\varepsilon_1\right) \\[4pt]
&= 230\,769.2 \times \left(-228 + 0.3 \times 1228\right)\times 10^{-6} \\[4pt]
&= 230\,769.2 \times \left(-228 + 368.4\right)\times 10^{-6} \\[4pt]
&= 230\,769.2 \times 140.4 \times 10^{-6} \\[4pt]
&\approx 32.4\;\text{MPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** $\boxed{\sigma_1 \approx 267.6\;\text{MPa},\quad \sigma_2 \approx 32.4\;\text{MPa}}$ *(7 marks: 3 for principal strain, 4 for principal stress)*

---

### Question B — (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 3]`

**(a)** With a neat schematic, describe the **steps involved in conducting a brittle lacquer test**, and explain how the **crack orientation** identifies the **direction of principal stresses**. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A steel component (E = 210 GPa, ν = 0.3) is coated with a brittle lacquer ($\varepsilon_c = 500\,\mu\varepsilon$, $\alpha_L = 80 \times 10^{-6}/^\circ$C). The test is performed in a hall where the temperature rises by $\Delta T = 5^\circ$C after coating. Will **spurious cracks** appear if $\alpha_{\text{steel}} = 12 \times 10^{-6}/^\circ$C? Also compute the **maximum principal stress** if strain gauges at a critical point record $\varepsilon_1 = 900\,\mu\varepsilon$ and $\varepsilon_2 = -100\,\mu\varepsilon$. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution — (a)

**Schematic description (3 marks):** (refer to Section 4.1 block diagram).
The steps are: surface preparation → primer coat → lacquer spray → controlled environment → incremental loading → observation under oblique light → photograph → trace principal directions.

**Crack orientation principle (4 marks):** The lacquer cracks wherever the maximum principal tensile strain on the substrate surface exceeds the lacquer's threshold strain $\varepsilon_c$. Brittle-fracture mechanics shows that a Mode I crack propagates normal to the direction of the maximum tensile stress. Therefore:

- Crack line is **parallel** to the minimum principal stress direction ($\sigma_2$).
- Crack normal is **perpendicular** to the maximum principal stress direction ($\sigma_1$).
- A network of *parallel* cracks indicates uniaxial tension; an *isoclinal* family of intersecting cracks indicates a biaxial stress field.

#### Model Solution — (b)

**Step 1 — Spurious crack check:**

$$
\varepsilon_{\text{thermal}} = (\alpha_m - \alpha_L)\Delta T = (12 - 80)\times 10^{-6} \times 5 = -340\,\mu\varepsilon
$$

Since the *magnitude* $|\varepsilon_{\text{thermal}}| = 340\,\mu\varepsilon < \varepsilon_c = 500\,\mu\varepsilon$, **no spurious cracks** are expected from the thermal excursion. *(2 marks)*

**Step 2 — Principal stress:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_1 &= \frac{E}{1-\nu^2}\left(\varepsilon_1 + \nu\,\varepsilon_2\right) \\[4pt]
&= \frac{210\,000}{0.91}\left(900 - 0.3 \times 100\right)\times 10^{-6} \\[4pt]
&= 230\,769.2 \times 870 \times 10^{-6} \\[4pt]
&\approx 200.8\;\text{MPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_2 &= \frac{E}{1-\nu^2}\left(\varepsilon_2 + \nu\,\varepsilon_1\right) \\[4pt]
&= 230\,769.2 \times \left(-100 + 0.3 \times 900\right)\times 10^{-6} \\[4pt]
&= 230\,769.2 \times 170 \times 10^{-6} \\[4pt]
&\approx 39.2\;\text{MPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** $\boxed{\sigma_1 \approx 200.8\;\text{MPa},\quad \sigma_2 \approx 39.2\;\text{MPa}}$ *(5 marks: 2 for spurious-crack check, 3 for principal stress)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls (Read Carefully):**
> 1. **Sign error in $\varepsilon_{\text{thermal}}$:** Most students forget the *order* $(\alpha_m - \alpha_L)$ — it must be the substrate minus the lacquer. If $\alpha_L > \alpha_m$, the *contraction* of the lacquer relative to the metal is what produces tensile parasitic strain.
> 2. **Crack direction confusion:** Students write "cracks form *along* the principal stress" — this loses 1 mark. The correct phrase is "cracks form *perpendicular* to the maximum principal stress."
> 3. **Forgetting to divide by 2 in the strain rosette formula** when computing principal strains. Always show the step: $(\varepsilon_x - \varepsilon_y)/2$ and $\gamma_{xy}/2$.
