- Eliminates **glare and hot spots** that would distort micrographs.
- Ensures **reproducible brightness** — micrographs taken weeks apart can be quantitatively compared.
- Required for **legally admissible images** under most chain-of-custody standards (SWGFAST/ENFSI guidelines).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls to AVOID:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the 0.61 in Abbe's equation.** Many students write $d = \frac{\lambda}{NA}$ (Rayleigh's approximation). For an objective with finite aperture, the rigorous Abbe form is $d = \frac{0.61 \lambda}{NA}$. Losing **1 mark**.
> 2. **Confusing total magnification with eyepiece magnification alone.** The product $M_o \times M_e$ is mandatory. Saying "10×" without specifying both is incomplete. Losing **1–2 marks**.
> 3. **Writing "USB" for a Wi-Fi microscope** or vice versa in the architectural diagrams. Examiners check the data-link layer carefully. Losing **1 mark**.
> 4. **Omitting the near-point distance (250 mm)** in the magnification derivation. KTU expects the standard convention. Losing **1 mark**.
> 5. **Failing to state the useful magnification range** when commenting on empty magnification. Always cite the rule $500 \cdot NA \le M \le 1000 \cdot NA$. Losing **1 mark**.
> 6. **Forgetting that reflected microscopy needs an opaque specimen.** Transmitted illumination cannot work on metallic or dense particulate evidence. Losing **1 mark**.
> 7. **Writing "magnification" instead of "resolution"** in the Abbe's equation context. These are inversely related but distinct concepts. Losing **0.5–1 mark**.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Bullet Summary — Read this 5 minutes before the exam.**

- [x] **Forensic Optical Microscopy** = visible-light microscopy applied to trace evidence; non-destructive, mandatory first step.
- [x] **Examination Workflow** = setup → mounting → observation → comparison → reporting.
- [x] **Magnification Formula** = $M_{tot} = M_{obj} \times M_{eye} = \frac{L \times D}{f_o \times f_e}$ with $L = 160$ mm, $D = 250$ mm.
- [x] **Abbe's Resolution Limit** = $d = \frac{0.61 \lambda}{NA}$ — the *theoretical floor* of detail.
- [x] **Numerical Aperture** = $NA = n \sin \alpha$ — higher NA = sharper image.
- [x] **Useful Magnification Rule** = $500 \cdot NA \le M \useful \le 1000 \cdot NA$.
- [x] **Empty Magnification** = $M > 1000 \cdot NA$ — image is bigger but blurrier.
- [x] **Depth of Field** = $\frac{n \lambda}{NA^2} + \frac{1000}{M_{tot} \cdot NA}$ μm.
- [x] **USB Microscope** = CMOS/CCD + USB 2.0/3.0 + host PC; magnification 10×–200× typical; lab-based.
- [x] **Wi-Fi Microscope** = wireless 802.11 + battery + smartphone/tablet; field-deployable; multi-user live view.
- [x] **Reflected (Epi-Illumination) Microscopy** = 45° beam splitter; objective is its own condenser; for opaque specimens.
- [x] **Köhler Illumination** = uniform, glare-free, reproducible; required for court-admissible micrographs.
- [x] **Forensic Evidence Classes** = fibres (transmitted), paint (reflected), hair (transmitted), tool marks (reflected), glass (both), drugs (polarized transmitted), documents (reflected + UV).
- [x] **Sensor vs Optical Resolution** = $R_{effective} = \max(R_{sensor}, R_{optical})$; NA matters more than megapixels.
- [x] **Working Distance** $\uparrow$ as NA $\downarrow$ — choose NA carefully to access recessed evidence.
- [x] **Calibration Rule** = Use a stage micrometer to calibrate the ocular/sensor scale before every forensic session.
- [x] **Forensic Advantage of Wi-Fi** = Real-time remote expert consultation + on-site evidence documentation.
- [x] **Reflected Microscopy Limitation** = Cannot examine transparent specimens; resolution of surface features only.
- [x] **Compound Microscope vs USB Microscope** = Compound = eyepiece-based, higher magnification, lab-grade; USB = sensor-based, lower magnification, field-grade.
- [x] **Half-NA Aperture** = Objective diaphragms must be opened to ~70% of NA for best image contrast.
- [x] **Airy Disc** = Diffraction pattern of a point; Rayleigh criterion = centre-to-centre distance $\ge$ Airy radius.
- [x] **NA hierarchy (medium)** = Air (1.00) < Water (1.33) < Glycerol (1.47) < Oil (1.515).
- [x] **Forensic micrograph rule** = Always include a *scale bar* and *magnification label* on the image.
- [x] **Reflected light path** = Source → mirror (reflect down) → objective → specimen → objective → mirror (transmit) → eyepiece.
- [x] **Crime scene priority** = Wi-Fi microscope for first-pass, lab compound microscope for court-grade verification.
- [x] **Key skill tested in KTU** = Ability to calculate $M_{tot}$, $d_{min}$, and classify magnification as useful or empty.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Novel Tools and System - Contour Method - Flash Thermography
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECET724 - FORENSIC ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Novel Tools and System - Contour Method - Flash Thermography

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 The Contour Method

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)**
> The **Contour Method** is a *destructive*, experimentally validated residual stress measurement technique in which a specimen is sectioned along a plane of interest, the resulting out-of-plane displacement (surface contour) caused by stress relaxation is precisely measured, and the original normal residual stress perpendicular to the cut plane is back-calculated using a **linear elastic Finite Element Analysis (FEA)** by enforcing the measured displacement field as a boundary condition.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The Contour Method was developed by **Dr. Michael B. Prime** at the **Los Alamos National Laboratory (LANL)** in the year **2000**. It is a KTU-specified "Novel Tool" because it is one of the few methods capable of producing a **full 2D map** of a single stress component across an entire cross-section, not just a 1D line profile.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Imagine you have a tightly coiled spring locked inside a solid block of transparent jelly. You cannot see the spring, but it pushes the jelly outward. Now, slice the jelly cleanly down the middle with a sharp, frictionless wire. The moment the cut is made, the invisible spring is released on one side, and the two halves of jelly shift *very slightly* — the cut face becomes wavy, no longer perfectly flat. By measuring that tiny waviness with a profilometer and feeding the wave shape back into a computer, an engineer can mathematically reconstruct exactly how hard and in what direction the "invisible spring" was pushing. That invisible spring represents the **locked-in residual stress**.

### 1.2 Flash Thermography

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)**
> **Flash Thermography** is a *non-destructive evaluation (NDE)* technique that uses a high-energy, short-duration pulse of light (typically from **Xenon flash lamps**) to uniformly heat the surface of a specimen, while an **infrared (IR) thermal camera** records the transient surface temperature decay. Subsurface anomalies such as delaminations, cracks, inclusions, and water ingress disrupt the heat flow, producing localized **thermal contrasts** that reveal hidden defects.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The pulse duration is typically between **1 ms to 15 ms**, and the energy density is around **5–50 kJ/m²**. The standard thermal diffusivity α of materials is benchmarked in the range **α ≈ 10⁻⁵ m²/s** for engineering alloys.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Think of a metal panel painted dark on one side. You take a high-power camera flash photo of it. The surface heats up instantly. Now watch it cool with a thermal camera. Wherever there is a hidden crack or air pocket just below the surface, that spot cools *more slowly* because the trapped air insulates the heat — so it appears as a **bright hot spot** in the IR image long after the rest has cooled. Flash thermography is essentially "X-ray vision through heat," but instead of using X-rays, it uses the *time it takes for heat to escape* as the diagnostic signal.

### 1.3 Why These Two Tools are Grouped as "Novel Forensic Tools"

| Tool | Destructive? | Stress Component Measured | Forensic Use Case |
|------|--------------|--------------------------|--------------------|
| Contour Method | Yes (Destructive) | Normal stress σₙ on cut plane | Welding residual stress, failure origin analysis |
| Flash Thermography | No (NDE) | None (defect detection) | Subsurface crack mapping in aircraft, composites, pipelines |

> [!TIP]
> In **forensic engineering**, the Contour Method is used when a component has **already failed** and investigators need to *quantify the residual stress* that contributed to the failure. Flash Thermography is used when a component is **still in service** and investigators need to *map hidden subsurface damage* without destroying evidence.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Contour Method — Operational Logic

The Contour Method rests on **Bueckner's Superposition Principle (1961)**. The four canonical steps are:

1. **Step 1 — Specimen Preparation:** A coupon of nominal dimensions (typical: 50 mm × 50 mm × 25 mm) is extracted from the component of interest.
2. **Step 2 — Wire EDM Cutting:** The specimen is sliced using **Wire Electrical Discharge Machining (Wire-EDM)** with a brass wire of ~0.25 mm diameter. The cut must be **low-stress, high-stability**, and produce a kerf width ≤ **0.30 mm**.
3. **Step 3 — Contour Measurement:** Each cut half is profiled using a **coordinate measuring machine (CMM)** with a touch-trigger probe (accuracy ~1–2 µm). The two contours are averaged to remove the kerf taper and EDM recast layer.
4. **Step 4 — FEA Back-Calculation:** The averaged 2D displacement contour is imported into FEA software (e.g., ABAQUS), applied as a *displacement boundary condition*, and the elastic stress required to flatten the surface is computed. This stress equals the **original residual stress** that was released.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Wire-EDM?** Wire-EDM cuts via localized melting and vaporization by spark erosion, with a **specific energy of ~2–4 J/mm³**. It introduces only **minimal additional residual stress** (typically < 20 MPa) compared to bandsaw cutting (which can introduce 100+ MPa). This is critical because the measurement resolution is governed by how "clean" the cut is.

### 2.2 Governing Equation — The Biharmonic Equation

The Contour Method is mathematically governed by the **biharmonic equation** of plane elasticity:

$$\nabla^{4} \phi = 0$$

where $\phi$ is the **Airy stress function** and $\nabla^{4}$ is the biharmonic operator. In expanded Cartesian form:

$$\frac{\partial^{4} \phi}{\partial x^{4}} + 2 \frac{\partial^{4} \phi}{\partial x^{2} \partial y^{2}} + \frac{\partial^{4} \phi}{\partial y^{4}} = 0$$

The stresses are recovered from:

$$\sigma_{x} = \frac{\partial^{2} \phi}{\partial y^{2}}, \quad \sigma_{y} = \frac{\partial^{2} \phi}{\partial x^{2}}, \quad \tau_{xy} = -\frac{\partial^{2} \phi}{\partial x \partial y}$$

The displacement field $u_{z}(x,y)$ (out-of-plane) is governed by:

$$u_{z}(x,y) = -\nu \int_{0}^{x} \int_{0}^{x'} \frac{\sigma_{y}}{E} \, dx'' \, dx' + \text{rigid body terms}$$

The inversion from displacement to stress is performed numerically by FEA.

### 2.3 Flash Thermography — Theoretical Foundation

Flash Thermography is governed by the **1-D transient heat conduction equation** (Fourier's law with no internal heat generation after the pulse):

$$\frac{\partial^{2} T}{\partial z^{2}} = \frac{1}{\alpha} \frac{\partial T}{\partial t}$$

with:

$$\alpha = \frac{k}{\rho c_{p}}$$

The **penetration depth** of the thermal wave at time $t$ is:

$$\delta = \sqrt{2 \alpha t}$$

The **surface temperature decay** for a semi-infinite solid (no defects) follows:

$$T(t) = \frac{Q_{0}}{\sqrt{\pi \rho c_{p} k t}}$$

where $Q_{0}$ is the absorbed energy per unit area (J/m²).

### 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| Symbol | Quantity | Formula / Expression | SI Unit |
|--------|----------|----------------------|---------|
| $\sigma_{x}, \sigma_{y}$ | Normal residual stresses | $\partial^{2}\phi / \partial y^{2}, \partial^{2}\phi / \partial x^{2}$ | Pa |
| $\tau_{xy}$ | Shear residual stress | $-\partial^{2}\phi / \partial x \partial y$ | Pa |
| $\phi$ | Airy stress function | Biharmonic solution of $\nabla^{4}\phi = 0$ | N·m/m |
| $u_{z}$ | Out-of-plane displacement (measured) | From CMM contour data | m |
| $E$ | Young's modulus | Material property | Pa |
| $\nu$ | Poisson's ratio | Material property | dimensionless |
| $\alpha$ | Thermal diffusivity | $k / (\rho c_{p})$ | m²/s |
| $\delta$ | Thermal penetration depth | $\sqrt{2 \alpha t}$ | m |
| $T(t)$ | Surface temperature decay | $Q_{0} / \sqrt{\pi \rho c_{p} k t}$ | K |
| $Q_{0}$ | Absorbed flash energy | Per unit area | J/m² |
| $k$ | Thermal conductivity | Material property | W/(m·K) |
| $\rho$ | Density | Material property | kg/m³ |
| $c_{p}$ | Specific heat capacity | Material property | J/(kg·K) |
| $t$ | Time after flash | Experimentally sampled | s |

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world utility of the biharmonic equation**: It is the same equation that governs **slow viscous flow (Stokes flow)** and **plate bending (Kirchhoff-Love plate theory)**. This mathematical universality is why the FEA back-calculation in the Contour Method is computationally efficient and convergent.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-world utility of $\delta = \sqrt{2\alpha t}$**: In forensic thermography of **CFRP (Carbon Fiber Reinforced Polymer)** composites, $\alpha \approx 5 \times 10^{-6}$ m²/s, so at $t = 1$ s the thermal wave has penetrated $\delta \approx 3.16$ mm. A defect at 3 mm depth is detectable. This is why the operator must know the material's diffusivity *before* setting the camera frame rate.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the 1-D Thermal Decay (Flash Thermography)

We derive the surface temperature response of a **semi-infinite solid** subject to an instantaneous surface heat input $Q_{0}$ (J/m²) at $t = 0$.

**Step 1 — Governing Equation**

For 1-D heat conduction in the $z$-direction (depth) with no internal heat generation:

$$\frac{\partial T(z,t)}{\partial t} = \alpha \frac{\partial^{2} T(z,t)}{\partial z^{2}}$$

with the initial condition $T(z,0) = 0$ for $z > 0$.

**Step 2 — Boundary Conditions**

A pulse energy of $Q_{0}$ is deposited at the surface $z = 0$:

$$-k \left. \frac{\partial T}{\partial z} \right\vert_{z=0} = Q_{0} \delta(t)$$

and the solid is infinite in the $z$ direction, so $T(\infty, t) = 0$.

**Step 3 — Laplace Transform Solution**

Taking the Laplace transform in time with respect to $t$:

$$s \, \tilde{T}(z,s) = \alpha \frac{d^{2} \tilde{T}}{d z^{2}}$$

This is an ODE in $z$ with the general solution:

$$\tilde{T}(z,s) = A e^{-z \sqrt{s/\alpha}} + B e^{+z \sqrt{s/\alpha}}$$

Since $T(\infty, t) = 0$, we have $B = 0$. The surface BC gives:

$$-k \frac{d \tilde{T}}{d z} \bigg\vert_{z=0} = Q_{0} \quad \Rightarrow \quad A \cdot k \cdot \sqrt{\frac{s}{\alpha}} = Q_{0}$$

$$A = \frac{Q_{0}}{k} \sqrt{\frac{\alpha}{s}}$$

**Step 4 — Surface Temperature**

At $z = 0$:

$$\tilde{T}(0,s) = \frac{Q_{0}}{k} \sqrt{\frac{\alpha}{s}} = \frac{Q_{0} \sqrt{\alpha}}{k \sqrt{s}}$$

**Step 5 — Inverse Laplace Transform**

Using the standard transform pair $\mathcal{L}^{-1} \{ s^{-1/2} \} = 1 / \sqrt{\pi t}$:

$$T(0,t) = \frac{Q_{0} \sqrt{\alpha}}{k} \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{\pi t}} = \frac{Q_{0}}{\sqrt{\pi \rho c_{p} k t}}$$

Substituting $\alpha = k / (\rho c_{p})$ in the last step recovers the standard 1-D form. $\blacksquare$

### 3.2 Derivation of the Thermal Penetration Depth

Starting from the diffusion equation solution of the form:

$$T(z,t) \propto \exp\!\left(-\frac{z^{2}}{4\alpha t}\right)$$

we define $\delta$ as the depth at which the exponent equals $-1/2$ (the e-folding criterion):

$$\frac{\delta^{2}}{4 \alpha t} = \frac{1}{2} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \delta^{2} = 2 \alpha t$$

$$\boxed{\delta = \sqrt{2 \alpha t}} \quad \blacksquare$$

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — Flash Thermography on Aluminum

> **Problem:** An aluminum plate ($\rho = 2700$ kg/m³, $c_{p} = 900$ J/(kg·K), $k = 237$ W/(m·K)) is subjected to a flash of $Q_{0} = 15000$ J/m². Compute the surface temperature at $t = 1$ s and the thermal penetration depth.

**Step 1 — Compute diffusivity**

$$\alpha = \frac{k}{\rho c_{p}} = \frac{237}{2700 \times 900} = \frac{237}{2.43 \times 10^{6}} = 9.75 \times 10^{-5} \; \text{m}^{2}\!/\!\text{s}$$

**Step 2 — Surface temperature at $t = 1$ s**

$$T(0, 1) = \frac{15000}{\sqrt{\pi \times 2700 \times 900 \times 237 \times 1}}$$

Denominator: $\pi \times 2700 \times 900 \times 237 = 1.808 \times 10^{9}$, $\sqrt{1.808 \times 10^{9}} = 4.252 \times 10^{4}$

$$T(0, 1) = \frac{15000}{42520} = 0.353 \; \text{K}$$

**Step 3 — Penetration depth at $t = 1$ s**

$$\delta = \sqrt{2 \times 9.75 \times 10^{-5} \times 1} = \sqrt{1.95 \times 10^{-4}} = 1.396 \times 10^{-2} \; \text{m} \approx 13.96 \; \text{mm}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** In 1 second, the thermal wave has probed ~14 mm into the aluminum. A defect at this depth will appear as a thermal anomaly in the IR sequence. This is why **camera frame rate** must be selected to capture the early-time hot spots (typically **50–200 Hz** in the first 2 seconds).

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Contour Method Stress Recovery

The FEA back-calculation is performed in 4 algorithmic stages:

| Stage | Input | Operation | Output |
|-------|-------|-----------|--------|
| 1. Mesh | Geometry of cut face | Discretize into ~50,000 quadrilateral elements | FEA mesh |
| 2. BC Apply | Measured $u_{z}(x,y)$ | Nodal displacement constraint | Nodal DOF |
| 3. Solve | Material $E, \nu$ | Solve $\mathbf{K}\mathbf{u} = \mathbf{F}$ | Reaction forces |
| 4. Recover | Reaction nodal forces | $\sigma_{n} = F_{n}/A_{n}$ | Stress map $\sigma_{n}(x,y)$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Analogy to forensic science:** The measured displacement is the *fingerprint* of the residual stress. The FEA back-calculation is the *mathematical fingerprint database lookup*. The accuracy of the result depends on the *fidelity of the fingerprint* (i.e., the CMM measurement resolution) and the *size of the database* (i.e., the number of FEA elements).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Contour Method — Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[ComponentUnderAnalysis] --> B[Step1ExtractCoupon]
    B --> C[Step2WireEDMSectioning]
    C --> D[Step3aCMMProfileHalfA]
    C --> E[Step3bCMMProfileHalfB]
    D --> F[Step4AverageContour]
    E --> F
    F --> G[Step5ImportToFEA]
    G --> H[Step6ApplyDisplacementBC]
    H --> I[Step7SolveBiharmonic]
    I --> J[Step8RecoverNormalStress]
    J --> K[Output2DResidualStressMap]
    K --> L[ForensicComparisonWithFailure]
```

### 4.2 Flash Thermography — Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[SpecimenSurface] --> B[Step1XenonFlashLamp]
    B --> C[Step2UniformSurfaceHeating]
    C --> D[Step3IRCameraRecording]
    D --> E[Step4ThermalSequenceCapture]
    E --> F[Step5PixelwiseTemperatureDecay]
    F --> G[Step6CompareToBaselineDecay]
    G --> H{DefectPresent}
    H -- Yes --> I[HotOrColdSpotDetected]
    H -- No --> J[UniformCooling]
    I --> K[Step7MapSubsurfaceDefect]
    K --> L[ForensicDefectReport]
    J --> M[NoDefectConcluded]
```

### 4.3 Combined Forensic Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[FailureOrAnomalyReported] --> B{ComponentIntact}
    B -- Yes --> C[ApplyFlashThermographyNDE]
    B -- No --> D[RecoverFailedComponent]
    C --> E[LocateSubsurfaceDefects]
    E --> F[DefectMapForensic]
    D --> G[ExtractCoupon]
    G --> H[ApplyContourMethod]
    H --> I[ComputeResidualStress]
    I --> J[CorrelateWithFailureStress]
    J --> K[RootCauseConclusion]
    F --> K
```

> [!TIP]
> The decision tree above is what KTU examiners expect students to draw in the **ESE (End Semester Examination)** when asked "How do you select between the Contour Method and Flash Thermography in a forensic investigation?" — the answer is governed by whether the component is still intact (NDE needed) or already broken (destructive sectioning possible).
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define the Contour Method of residual stress measurement. Who developed it and in which year?*

**Model Answer:**
The Contour Method is a destructive experimental technique used to measure the **2D distribution of normal residual stress** on an internal plane of a component. The procedure involves: (i) sectioning the component along the plane of interest using **low-stress wire-EDM**, (ii) precisely measuring the out-of-plane displacement contour of the two newly created surfaces using a **coordinate measuring machine (CMM)**, and (iii) back-calculating the original residual stress by applying the measured displacement as a boundary condition in a **linear elastic FEA** model.
It was developed by **Dr. Michael B. Prime** at the **Los Alamos National Laboratory (LANL), USA**, in the year **2000**. *(3 marks)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
*State the governing differential equation of the Contour Method and explain its physical meaning.*

**Model Answer:**
The governing equation is the **biharmonic equation of plane elasticity**:
$$\nabla^{4} \phi(x,y) = 0$$
where $\phi$ is the **Airy stress function** and $\nabla^{4}$ is the biharmonic operator. Physically, it states that for a body in **static equilibrium** under no body forces, the stress field must be *biharmonic* — meaning the function and its Laplacian are both harmonic. The stress components are recovered as second derivatives of $\phi$. *(3 marks)*

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> - Students commonly lose **2–3 marks** in the Contour Method derivation by forgetting to mention the **Airy stress function** and the fact that the **biharmonic equation requires the body to be in static equilibrium with no body forces**.
> - For Flash Thermography, students often write the **steady-state** heat equation instead of the **transient** diffusion equation — this is a **direct 2-mark deduction** in the ESE.
> - Always state **units** of every quantity you write on the answer sheet. Marks are reserved for unit consistency.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Contour Method Dominant**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** Describe the step-by-step procedure of the Contour Method for measuring residual stress in a metallic component. Why is **Wire-EDM** preferred over mechanical cutting? *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Derive the **biharmonic equation** $\nabla^{4}\phi = 0$ starting from the equilibrium equations of plane elasticity, and explain how it is used in the FEA back-calculation step. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Procedure (7 marks):**
1. **Coupon Extraction:** A representative specimen is cut from the component using a low-stress method such as band saw with coolant. *(1 mark)*
2. **Wire-EDM Sectioning:** The specimen is sliced along the plane of interest using **wire electrical discharge machining** with a thin (~0.25 mm) brass wire. *(1 mark)* Wire-EDM is preferred because: (i) it produces a **narrow kerf** (≤ 0.30 mm), (ii) it introduces **minimal additional residual stress** (< 20 MPa) because cutting is via spark erosion, not mechanical force, and (iii) it provides a **straight, repeatable cut** essential for accurate contour averaging. *(2 marks)*
3. **Contour Measurement:** Each cut face is profiled using a **CMM** with a touch-trigger probe at thousands of grid points with ~1 µm vertical accuracy. *(1 mark)*
4. **Averaging:** The two contours (from the two halves) are mirrored and averaged to remove the kerf taper and EDM recast layer. *(1 mark)*
5. **FEA Back-Calculation:** The averaged 2D displacement field is applied as a nodal boundary condition in a **3D linear elastic FEA** model. The reaction forces divided by elemental area give the **original residual stress**. *(1 mark)*

**(b) Biharmonic Derivation (7 marks):**
Starting from the **equilibrium equations** in 2D (no body forces):

$$\frac{\partial \sigma_{x}}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial \tau_{xy}}{\partial y} = 0$$
$$\frac{\partial \tau_{xy}}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial \sigma_{y}}{\partial y} = 0$$

Substituting the **strain-displacement relations** and **Hooke's Law** (plane stress), one obtains the **compatibility condition** which can be written as:

$$\frac{\partial^{2}}{\partial x^{2}}\!\left(\sigma_{y} - \nu \sigma_{x}\right) + \frac{\partial^{2}}{\partial y^{2}}\!\left(\sigma_{x} - \nu \sigma_{y}\right) = 2(1+\nu)\frac{\partial^{2} \tau_{xy}}{\partial x \partial y}$$

Introducing the **Airy stress function** $\phi$ such that:

$$\sigma_{x} = \frac{\partial^{2} \phi}{\partial y^{2}}, \quad \sigma_{y} = \frac{\partial^{2} \phi}{\partial x^{2}}, \quad \tau_{xy} = -\frac{\partial^{2} \phi}{\partial x \partial y}$$

these automatically satisfy equilibrium. Substituting into the compatibility equation gives:

$$\frac{\partial^{4} \phi}{\partial x^{4}} + 2 \frac{\partial^{4} \phi}{\partial x^{2} \partial y^{2}} + \frac{\partial^{4} \phi}{\partial y^{4}} = 0$$

$$\boxed{\nabla^{4} \phi = 0}$$

In the FEA back-calculation, the measured displacement $u_z$ is the **natural boundary data** of this biharmonic problem; solving $\nabla^{4}\phi = 0$ subject to the measured $u_z$ yields the **reaction stresses** that originally produced the observed contour. *(3 marks for the biharmonic appearance and the FEA link)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Flash Thermography Dominant**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** Explain the working principle of **Flash Thermography** as a non-destructive forensic tool. List the key equipment and state the typical flash energy density. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Derive the surface temperature decay expression $T(t) = Q_{0} / \sqrt{\pi \rho c_{p} k t}$ for a semi-infinite solid. Hence, compute the temperature after 2 s for a steel plate ($\rho = 7850$ kg/m³, $c_{p} = 490$ J/(kg·K), $k = 45$ W/(m·K)) subjected to a flash of $Q_{0} = 20000$ J/m². *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Working Principle (7 marks):**
Flash Thermography is a **transient, non-contact NDE** technique used in forensic investigations to detect **subsurface defects** (cracks, delaminations, inclusions, water ingress) in materials such as metals, composites, and concrete. *(1 mark)*

**Equipment:** *(3 marks)*
- **Xenon flash lamps** (typically 2–4 units, each rated at 6–8 kJ, firing in synchrony) to deliver a uniform heat pulse of 1–15 ms duration.
- **Infrared (IR) thermal camera** (e.g., InSb or MCT detector, spectral range 3–5 µm or 8–14 µm, NETD < 25 mK) recording at 50–200 Hz.
- **Triggering and synchronization unit** to time the flash with the camera acquisition.
- **Data acquisition and processing software** for thermographic sequence analysis.

**Working Mechanism:** The flash uniformly heats the surface to a few degrees above ambient. The IR camera records the **thermal decay sequence** over 1–10 seconds. Defective regions (e.g., a delamination) interrupt the 1-D heat flow, causing the local surface to cool **more slowly (hot spot)** or **more quickly (cold spot)** compared to sound regions. By comparing pixelwise decay to a baseline, defect location, size, and depth are inferred. *(2 marks)*

**Typical flash energy density:** **5 – 50 kJ/m²**. *(1 mark)*

**(b) Derivation and Calculation (7 marks):**
*(See Section 3.1 of these notes for the full derivation. Key steps:)*

**Step 1:** Write 1-D heat diffusion equation $\partial T / \partial t = \alpha \, \partial^{2}T/\partial z^{2}$ with $T(z,0) = 0$ and the surface flux BC: $-k \, \partial T/\partial z \vert_{z=0} = Q_{0}\delta(t)$. *(1 mark)*

**Step 2:** Laplace transform in time, solve the resulting ODE in $z$, and apply the BC at $z = 0$ to get $\tilde{T}(0,s) = Q_{0}\sqrt{\alpha}/(k\sqrt{s})$. *(2 marks)*

**Step 3:** Apply inverse Laplace transform using $\mathcal{L}^{-1}\{s^{-1/2}\} = 1/\sqrt{\pi t}$ to obtain:
$$T(0,t) = \frac{Q_{0}}{\sqrt{\pi \rho c_{p} k t}} \quad \blacksquare \quad (2 \text{ marks})$$

**Step 4 — Numerical computation (2 marks):**

$$\alpha = \frac{45}{7850 \times 490} = \frac{45}{3.8465 \times 10^{6}} = 1.170 \times 10^{-5} \; \text{m}^{2}\!/\!\text{s}$$

$$T(0, 2) = \frac{20000}{\sqrt{\pi \times 7850 \times 490 \times 45 \times 2}}$$

Inner product: $7850 \times 490 \times 45 \times 2 = 3.461 \times 10^{8}$
$\pi \times 3.461 \times 10^{8} = 1.087 \times 10^{9}$
$\sqrt{1.087 \times 10^{9}} = 3.297 \times 10^{4}$

$$T(0, 2) = \frac{20000}{32970} = 0.607 \; \text{K}$$

**Penetration depth check:**

$$\delta = \sqrt{2 \times 1.170 \times 10^{-5} \times 2} = \sqrt{4.68 \times 10^{-5}} = 6.84 \times 10^{-3} \; \text{m} \approx 6.84 \; \text{mm}$$

> The thermal wave has probed ~6.8 mm into the steel, so any defect in the top 6 mm of the steel plate will produce a visible thermal anomaly in the IR sequence.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ The **Contour Method** is a **destructive** technique developed by **Michael Prime (LANL, 2000)**; it measures the **normal stress** component on a cut plane.
- ✅ The cut is performed with **Wire-EDM** to avoid introducing **extraneous residual stress** that would contaminate the measurement.
- ✅ The **CMM** measures the out-of-plane displacement with **~1 µm accuracy**; the two contours are averaged to remove kerf and recast artifacts.
- ✅ The governing equation is the **biharmonic equation** $\nabla^{4}\phi = 0$ with $\phi$ as the **Airy stress function**.
- ✅ The Contour Method yields a **2D map** of a single stress component, not a 1D line profile.
- ✅ **Flash Thermography** is a **non-destructive, transient, non-contact** technique using **Xenon flash lamps** (1–15 ms pulse) and an **IR camera**.
- ✅ The flash energy density is **5 – 50 kJ/m²** and camera frame rate is typically **50–200 Hz**.
- ✅ The governing equation is the **1-D transient heat diffusion equation** $\partial T/\partial t = \alpha \, \partial^{2}T/\partial z^{2}$.
- ✅ The **thermal penetration depth** is $\delta = \sqrt{2 \alpha t}$; this defines the **maximum detectable defect depth** for a given observation time.
- ✅ The **surface temperature decay** for a sound region follows $T(t) = Q_{0}/\sqrt{\pi \rho c_{p} k t}$.
- ✅ Defects appear as **hot spots (delamination, low-$\alpha$ inclusion)** or **cold spots (high-$\alpha$ inclusion like water)** in the IR sequence.
- ✅ In **forensic engineering**, choose **Contour Method** when the component is already failed and stress quantification is needed; choose **Flash Thermography** when the component is intact and subsurface defect mapping is required.
- ✅ Always cite **units** in KTU answers: residual stress in **Pa or MPa**, thermal diffusivity in **m²/s**, temperature in **K or °C**, and contour displacement in **µm**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Exam Tip:** The 2024 Scheme emphasizes *visualization* and *flowchart-style reasoning*. Always close a long-answer question with a one-sentence summary line, e.g., *"Thus, the Contour Method and Flash Thermography together provide a complete forensic toolkit — the former quantifying locked-in stress, the latter locating hidden damage."* Examiners award the **final 1 mark** for synthesis statements.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Thermographic Signal Reconstruction (TSR) - Electromagnetically Induced Acoustic Emission (EMAE) - Pulsed Eddy Current (PEA) - Theory Only
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECET724 - FORENSIC ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Thermographic Signal Reconstruction (TSR) - Electromagnetically Induced Acoustic Emission (EMAE) - Pulsed Eddy Current (PEA) - Theory Only

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definitions & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Thermographic Signal Reconstruction (TSR)

### Formal Academic Definition
**Thermographic Signal Reconstruction (TSR)** is an advanced, mathematically rigorous post-processing algorithm applied to raw time-dependent thermal data acquired during **Pulsed Thermography (PT)**. It was originally developed by **Stephen M. Shepard** at NASA Langley Research Center. The technique reconstructs the noise-free underlying thermal response of a test specimen by fitting a low-order logarithmic polynomial to the temperature-time history of every pixel in the thermal image sequence. This reconstruction dramatically improves the signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) and enhances the visibility of subsurface defects.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** TSR is purely a *post-processing* data reduction technique — it does **not** acquire new data; it mathematically refines existing data to extract defect information that is otherwise buried in noise.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Imagine you are standing in a crowded railway station trying to listen to an announcement. The raw thermal data is like that announcement mixed with the noise of trains, chatter, and announcements. **TSR is like a noise-cancelling headphone algorithm**: it learns the underlying "signal pattern" of the noise and subtracts it, leaving a clean, intelligible signal. In the same way, TSR learns the smooth thermal decay curve of a healthy pixel and removes random noise — the defect shows up as a clear *deviation* from the smooth curve.

### The Three Fundamental TSR Image Products
For every pixel, TSR generates three distinct images:

1. **Raw Image** — The unprocessed thermal contrast from the original camera output.
2. **1st Time Derivative Image** — The rate of temperature change with time. Sharply highlights defect edges.
3. **2nd Time Derivative Image** — The acceleration of cooling. Maximizes contrast for deep, subtle defects.

### Standard Metric
The logarithmic polynomial used in TSR is typically of the **4th, 5th, 6th, 7th, 8th, or 9th order**, with **7th or 8th order** being the industry standard for NDT applications.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Physical Insight:** TSR assumes a one-dimensional, semi-infinite heat conduction model. Therefore, it works exceptionally well for thin laminates, composites, and metallic plates, but its accuracy degrades for complex 3D geometries.

---

## 1.2 Electromagnetically Induced Acoustic Emission (EMAE)

### Formal Academic Definition
**Electromagnetically Induced Acoustic Emission (EMAE)** is a hybrid non-destructive testing (NDT) technique that combines the principles of **electromagnetic induction** with **acoustic emission (AE)**. In EMAE, a time-varying electromagnetic field (typically generated by a coil carrying a pulsed or modulated current) interacts with a conductive material to produce local **Lorentz forces**. These forces induce elastic (acoustic) waves that propagate through the material and are detected by piezoelectric transducers mounted on the specimen. The technique is used to detect and characterize **surface and near-surface defects** in both **ferromagnetic and non-ferromagnetic** conductive materials.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Think of holding a powerful magnet near a metal spring. The magnet exerts a force on the spring, and if you modulate (turn on/off) the magnet, the spring vibrates and you can hear a faint hum. **EMAE works exactly like this**: instead of a permanent magnet, we use a pulsed electromagnet (a coil). When switched rapidly, the resulting Lorentz force makes the metal "vibrate" — emitting sound. **Defects** act like a kink in the spring: they vibrate differently, producing a different "sound" that our sensors can hear.

### The Two Physical Mechanisms
EMAE operates through **two distinct physical mechanisms**, depending on the material:

1. **Lorentz Force Mechanism** — Dominant in non-ferromagnetic materials (aluminum, copper, austenitic stainless steel). The induced eddy currents interact with the magnetic field to produce a force **J × B** on the material lattice.
2. **Magnetostrictive Mechanism** — Dominant in ferromagnetic materials (carbon steel, iron, nickel). The magnetic field causes microscopic dimensional changes in the crystal lattice, generating stress waves.

### Standard Metric
- **Operating Frequency Range:** Typically **20 kHz to 1 MHz** for the induced acoustic waves.
- **Electromagnetic Excitation Frequency:** Usually below **10 kHz** (audio frequency range).
- **Transducer Type:** Wideband piezoelectric AE sensors (commonly resonant at **150 kHz** or **500 kHz**).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** EMAE is often grouped with EMAT (Electromagnetic Acoustic Transducer) as a contact-free or minimal-contact technique. However, EMAE specifically uses *acoustic emission* (passive listening to defects) while EMAT uses *active transmission*.

---

## 1.3 Pulsed Eddy Current (PEA / PEC)

### Formal Academic Definition
**Pulsed Eddy Current (PEC)** testing is an advanced variant of conventional Eddy Current Testing (ECT) in which the excitation coil is energized with a **broadband pulsed square wave** instead of a single-frequency sinusoidal signal. Because a single pulse contains a **broad spectrum of frequencies** (as described by Fourier theory), a single PEC scan can simultaneously probe multiple depths within a specimen. The response is analyzed in the **time domain**, where the decay of the induced eddy currents is monitored. The rate of decay is a strong function of **wall thickness**, **conductivity**, and the presence of **sub-surface defects** (especially corrosion).

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Imagine throwing a stone into a pond. The ripple is a single "pulse" containing waves of all sizes. A **conventional eddy current** is like throwing a single-size wave — you only see one depth. **Pulsed eddy current is like throwing the stone**: the big waves probe deep, the small ripples probe the surface. By measuring how fast the ripple dies down, you can tell how deep the water is and whether there is a rock (defect) hiding below the surface. In industrial terms, PEC can detect **Corrosion Under Insulation (CUI)** without removing the insulation — a massive cost saving in oil & gas, petrochemical, and power industries.

### The Time-Domain PEC Signal
A typical PEC response has three characteristic time regions:

1. **Early Time (Rise Phase)** — Dominated by **lift-off** (probe-to-specimen distance) and surface features.
2. **Mid Time** — Sensitive to **intermediate depth** defects and wall thickness.
3. **Late Time (Decay Phase)** — Contains information about **deep defects, far-side corrosion**, and bulk wall thickness.

### Standard Metric
- **Pulse Repetition Rate:** Typically **100 Hz to 1 kHz**.
- **Pulse Width:** Usually **1 ms to 10 ms**.
- **Probe Diameter:** Range from **10 mm** to over **100 mm** for large area inspection.
- **Liftoff:** Can tolerate lift-offs of up to **100 mm** when scanning through insulation.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Application Highlight:** PEC is the *industry gold standard* for inspecting insulated piping, vessel shells, and tank floors. It saves millions of dollars by eliminating the need for scaffolding and insulation removal during routine CUI surveys.

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Deep Theoretical Analysis of TSR

### The Mathematical Foundation
TSR is built on the **1-D heat conduction equation** for a semi-infinite solid subjected to a uniform instantaneous heat pulse (Dirac delta). The analytical solution gives the surface temperature decay as:

$$\theta(t) = \frac{Q}{e\sqrt{\pi t}}$$

where:
- $\theta(t)$ is the surface temperature rise above ambient at time $t$
- $Q$ is the absorbed heat energy per unit area
- $e$ is the **thermal effusivity** (a key material property)
- $t$ is the elapsed time after the flash

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why effusivity matters:** $e = \sqrt{k \rho c_p}$, where $k$ is thermal conductivity, $\rho$ is density, and $c_p$ is specific heat. Effusivity governs how rapidly heat flows into a material.

### The Logarithmic Polynomial Reconstruction
Because real cameras sample discrete data with noise, the **raw** $\theta(t)$ cannot be used directly. Shepard proposed fitting the **natural logarithm** of temperature vs. natural logarithm of time with a polynomial:

$$\ln(\theta) = a_0 + a_1 \ln(t) + a_2 [\ln(t)]^2 + a_3 [\ln(t)]^3 + \cdots + a_n [\ln(t)]^n$$

### The Three Step-by-Step Operational Steps
1. **Step 1 — Logarithmic Domain Conversion:** Convert each pixel's temperature-time history to $\ln(\theta)$ vs $\ln(t)$. This linearizes the pure $1/\sqrt{t}$ decay.
2. **Step 2 — Polynomial Fitting:** Use **least-squares regression** to fit an n-th order polynomial to the log-transformed data. The fitted coefficients $a_0, a_1, \ldots, a_n$ describe the smooth, noise-free underlying thermal response.
3. **Step 3 — Derivative Image Generation:** Compute the **first** and **second** time derivatives of the fitted polynomial with respect to $\ln(t)$. Display these as images to reveal defects with maximum contrast.

### The Derivative Formulas
If $T(\ln t)$ is the fitted logarithmic polynomial, then:

$$\frac{dT}{d(\ln t)} = a_1 + 2 a_2 \ln(t) + 3 a_3 [\ln(t)]^2 + \cdots + n a_n [\ln(t)]^{n-1}$$

$$\frac{d^2 T}{d(\ln t)^2} = 2 a_2 + 6 a_3 \ln(t) + 12 a_4 [\ln(t)]^2 + \cdots + n(n-1) a_n [\ln(t)]^{n-2}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why derivatives work:** A subsurface defect acts as a thermal barrier. It causes a small but distinct *kink* or change in slope in the cooling curve. Taking derivatives amplifies these slope changes, making tiny defects visible.

### Real-World Engineering Utility
- **Aerospace:** Detects delamination, water ingress, and impact damage in composite aircraft structures.
- **Civil Engineering:** Detects voids, delamination, and rebar corrosion in concrete bridge decks.
- **Power Generation:** Inspects turbine blades, composite fan blades, and rotor components.

---

## 2.2 Deep Theoretical Analysis of EMAE

### The Lorentz Force Mechanism (Non-Ferromagnetic Materials)
When a coil carrying a current density $\mathbf{J_c}$ is placed near a conductive specimen carrying induced eddy currents $\mathbf{J_e}$ in a magnetic field $\mathbf{B}$, the **Lorentz force per unit volume** is:

$$\mathbf{F_L} = \mathbf{J_e} \times \mathbf{B}$$

This force acts on the lattice and produces mechanical displacement. If the current is pulsed or modulated at frequency $f$, the force oscillates at $f$, launching elastic waves into the material at the same frequency.

### The Magnetostrictive Mechanism (Ferromagnetic Materials)
In ferromagnetic materials, the **magnetostrictive strain** $S$ is a function of the applied magnetic field $H$:

$$S = f(H)$$

For small variations around an operating point, this is approximated as a linear relation with the magnetostrictive coefficient $d$:

$$\Delta S = d \cdot \Delta H$$

This dimensional change in the lattice acts as a direct source of elastic wave generation.

### The Two Operational Modes
1. **Continuous Wave Mode (CW-EMAE):** A sinusoidal current is applied; defect-induced perturbations are detected as changes in the received acoustic signal.
2. **Pulsed Mode (P-EMAE):** A short current pulse is applied; the **acoustic emission** from the material is monitored as it "rings down" — defects produce characteristic acoustic signatures during the ringdown.

### Real-World Engineering Utility
- **Pipeline Inspection:** Detects SCC (Stress Corrosion Cracking) in austenitic stainless steel pipelines.
- **Nuclear Industry:** Inspects welds in stainless steel reactor components where conventional UT is difficult.
- **Weld Inspection:** Detects HAZ (Heat-Affected Zone) cracks in ferritic and austenitic welds.

---

## 2.3 Deep Theoretical Analysis of PEA (PEC)

### The Underlying Physics — Faraday's Law of Induction
A time-varying current $I(t)$ in the excitation coil produces a time-varying magnetic field $\mathbf{B}(t)$, which by **Faraday's Law** induces eddy currents in the specimen:

$$\nabla \times \mathbf{E} = -\frac{\partial \mathbf{B}}{\partial t}$$

The eddy current density $\mathbf{J_e}$ in the specimen decays exponentially with depth, characterized by the **standard depth of penetration** $\delta$:

$$\delta = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\pi f \mu \sigma}}$$

where:
- $f$ is the excitation frequency
- $\mu$ is the magnetic permeability of the specimen
- $\sigma$ is the electrical conductivity

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Concept:** A pulse contains many frequencies. **Low frequencies** give a large $\delta$ (deep penetration); **high frequencies** give a small $\delta$ (surface-sensitive). The pulsed approach therefore gives depth information automatically.

### The Time-Domain Response of PEC
When the excitation pulse is suddenly switched off, the eddy current in the specimen does not vanish instantly. It decays exponentially. The magnetic field from this decaying eddy current is detected by a pickup (sense) coil. The detected voltage $V(t)$ follows:

$$V(t) = V_0 \cdot e^{-t/\tau}$$

where $\tau$ is the **time constant** of the system, related to wall thickness $d$, permeability $\mu$, and conductivity $\sigma$:

$$\tau \approx \frac{\mu \sigma d^2}{\pi^2}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Measuring the decay time $\tau$ directly gives the **wall thickness** $d$ — even when the wall is covered with insulation, paint, or cladding. This is the basis of modern CUI inspection tools.

### The Three Operationally Distinct Time Windows
1. **Early Time Window:** Signal is dominated by **lift-off**. Used as a baseline reference.
2. **Mid Time Window:** Sensitive to **wall thickness** changes; ideal for general corrosion mapping.
3. **Late Time Window:** Sensitive to **far-side** defects and very thick walls. Used for deep corrosion detection.

### Real-World Engineering Utility
- **Oil & Gas:** Corrosion Under Insulation (CUI) screening of process piping.
- **Power Plants:** Boiler tube inspection and steam header wall thickness measurement.
- **Aerospace:** Multi-layer lap joint inspection (e.g., fuselage skins).
- **Shipbuilding:** Hull plate thickness surveys without removing anti-fouling coatings.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Symbol / Concept** | **Equation / Definition** | **Physical Meaning** | **Units** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Thermal Effusivity $e$ | $e = \sqrt{k \rho c_p}$ | Ability of material to exchange thermal energy | $J \cdot m^{-2} \cdot K^{-1} \cdot s^{-1/2}$ |
| TSR Logarithmic Polynomial | $\ln(\theta) = a_0 + a_1 \ln(t) + a_2 [\ln(t)]^2 + \ldots + a_n [\ln(t)]^n$ | Fits noisy decay data | Dimensionless |
| TSR 1st Derivative | $dT/d(\ln t) = a_1 + 2 a_2 \ln(t) + \ldots + n a_n [\ln(t)]^{n-1}$ | Cooling rate image | K |
| TSR 2nd Derivative | $d^2T/d(\ln t)^2 = 2 a_2 + 6 a_3 \ln(t) + \ldots$ | Cooling acceleration image | K |
| Lorentz Force Density | $\mathbf{F_L} = \mathbf{J_e} \times \mathbf{B}$ | Force on eddy-current carrying conductor | $N \cdot m^{-3}$ |
| Magnetostrictive Strain | $\Delta S = d \cdot \Delta H$ | Strain in ferromagnet due to field change | Dimensionless |
| Faraday's Law | $\nabla \times \mathbf{E} = -\partial \mathbf{B} / \partial t$ | Induction of EMF by changing flux | $V \cdot m^{-1}$ |
| Standard Depth of Penetration | $\delta = 1 / \sqrt{\pi f \mu \sigma}$ | Depth of eddy current penetration | m |
| PEC Decay Time Constant | $\tau \approx \mu \sigma d^2 / \pi^2$ | Time for eddy current to decay | s |
| PEC Detector Voltage | $V(t) = V_0 \cdot e^{-t/\tau}$ | Sense coil voltage decay | V |
| EMAE Lorentz Frequency | $f_{acoustic} = f_{excitation}$ | Acoustic frequency equals excitation | Hz |
| Pulse Repetition Rate | $f_{PRR} = 1/T_{pulse}$ | Pulses per second | Hz |
| Maximum Lift-off | $d_{lift-off,max}$ up to $100 \, mm$ | Probe-to-specimen gap tolerance | mm |

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: The Theoretical Surface Temperature Decay (TSR Foundation)

### Starting Point — 1D Heat Conduction Equation
For a semi-infinite solid, the heat equation is:

$$\frac{\partial^2 T}{\partial x^2} = \frac{1}{\alpha} \frac{\partial T}{\partial t}$$

where $\alpha = k / (\rho c_p)$ is the **thermal diffusivity**.

### Boundary and Initial Conditions
The surface is subjected to an instantaneous heat pulse of energy $Q$ per unit area at $t = 0$. Using an **error function (Gaussian) solution**, the surface temperature response is:

$$\theta(t) = \frac{Q}{e\sqrt{\pi t}}$$

### Step-by-Step Derivation to the Logarithmic Form
We need to express this in the form $\ln(\theta)$ vs $\ln(t)$ to understand the TSR polynomial fit.

**Step 1:** Take the natural logarithm of both sides:

$$\ln[\theta(t)] = \ln\left(\frac{Q}{e\sqrt{\pi t}}\right)$$

**Step 2:** Apply the logarithm property $\ln(a \cdot b) = \ln a + \ln b$ and $\ln(1/\sqrt{t}) = -\frac{1}{2}\ln(t)$:

$$\ln[\theta(t)] = \ln(Q) - \ln(e) - \frac{1}{2}\ln(\pi) - \frac{1}{2}\ln(t)$$

**Step 3:** Group all constant terms into a single coefficient $a_0$:

$$a_0 = \ln(Q) - \ln(e) - \frac{1}{2}\ln(\pi)$$

**Step 4:** Note that the coefficient of $\ln(t)$ is $-\frac{1}{2}$:

$$a_1 = -\frac{1}{2}$$

**Step 5:** All higher-order coefficients $a_2, a_3, \ldots, a_n$ are zero for a **perfect, defect-free, semi-infinite, homogeneous** specimen:

$$\ln[\theta(t)] = a_0 + a_1 \ln(t)$$

**Step 6:** For a **real, defective** specimen, the higher-order coefficients become non-zero. They encode the defect information:

$$\ln[\theta(t)] = a_0 + a_1 \ln(t) + a_2 [\ln(t)]^2 + a_3 [\ln(t)]^3 + \cdots + a_n [\ln(t)]^n$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Engineering Insight:** A defect-free area has $a_2 = a_3 = \ldots = a_n = 0$. The **magnitude of these higher-order coefficients is directly proportional to the size and depth of the defect** beneath that pixel. This is why the 2nd derivative image (which depends on $a_2, a_3, \ldots$) reveals defects with maximum contrast.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: The Lorentz Force Density in EMAE

### Starting Point — Maxwell's Equations
A current-carrying coil produces a magnetic field $\mathbf{B}$:

$$\mathbf{B} = \mu_0 \mu_r \mathbf{H}$$

In a conductive specimen, Faraday's law induces an electric field $\mathbf{E}$ and a corresponding eddy current density $\mathbf{J_e}$:

$$\mathbf{J_e} = \sigma \mathbf{E}$$

### Step-by-Step Derivation of the Force Equation

**Step 1:** The Lorentz force per unit charge is $\mathbf{F}/q = \mathbf{v} \times \mathbf{B}$. For a current density $\mathbf{J}$ (current per unit area), the force per unit volume is:

$$\mathbf{F_L} = \mathbf{J_e} \times \mathbf{B}$$

**Step 2:** Express the magnetic field from the coil using the Biot-Savart law for a coil of $N$ turns, length $L$, and current $I_c$:

$$B = \frac{\mu_0 N I_c}{L}$$

**Step 3:** Express the induced eddy current density using Faraday's law. For a coil of radius $r$ with a time-varying current $dI_c/dt$, the induced EMF in the specimen is:

$$\mathcal{E} = -M \frac{dI_c}{dt}$$

where $M$ is the mutual inductance between the coil and the eddy current loop.

**Step 4:** The eddy current density in the specimen of conductivity $\sigma$ and path length $\ell$:

$$J_e = \frac{\sigma \mathcal{E}}{\ell} = -\frac{\sigma M}{\ell} \frac{dI_c}{dt}$$

**Step 5:** Substitute into the Lorentz force equation:

$$\mathbf{F_L} = -\frac{\sigma M}{\ell} \frac{dI_c}{dt} \times \mathbf{B}$$

**Step 6:** For a sinusoidal current $I_c(t) = I_0 \sin(\omega t)$, the time derivative is $dI_c/dt = \omega I_0 \cos(\omega t)$. Therefore the force oscillates at frequency $\omega$:

$$F_L(t) = \frac{\sigma M B \omega I_0}{\ell} \cos(\omega t)$$

**Step 7:** This oscillating force launches an **elastic wave** of the same frequency into the material. The wave propagates at the material's sound velocity:

$$v_{sound} = \sqrt{\frac{E}{\rho}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Defect Detection Mechanism:** When a defect (e.g., a crack) is present, it locally **interrupts the eddy current path** and **disrupts the Lorentz force distribution**. This changes the amplitude, phase, and frequency content of the received acoustic signal. The crack essentially becomes an "acoustic reflector" or "acoustic source," depending on its orientation relative to the force vector.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: The PEC Time-Domain Decay

### Starting Point — Diffusion Equation for Eddy Currents
The eddy current in a thick plate satisfies the diffusion equation:

$$\nabla^2 \mathbf{J_e} = \mu \sigma \frac{\partial \mathbf{J_e}}{\partial t}$$

### Step-by-Step Derivation of the Decay Time Constant

**Step 1:** For a 1D problem where eddy currents flow in the $y$-direction and decay with depth $x$, the equation simplifies to:

$$\frac{\partial^2 J_y}{\partial x^2} = \mu \sigma \frac{\partial J_y}{\partial t}$$

**Step 2:** At the moment the excitation pulse is switched off ($t = 0$), the initial eddy current distribution $J_y(x, 0)$ can be expanded in a Fourier sine series (since $J_y$ must vanish at $x = 0$ for an insulated surface):

$$J_y(x, 0) = \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} A_n \sin\left(\frac{n \pi x}{d}\right)$$

where $d$ is the plate thickness.

**Step 3:** The solution to the diffusion equation with this initial condition is:

$$J_y(x, t) = \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} A_n \sin\left(\frac{n \pi x}{d}\right) \exp\left(-\frac{n^2 \pi^2 t}{\mu \sigma d^2}\right)$$

**Step 4:** Define the **time constant** $\tau$ for the fundamental mode ($n = 1$):

$$\tau = \frac{\mu \sigma d^2}{\pi^2}$$

**Step 5:** The fundamental mode (n=1) dominates the late-time behavior. The sense coil voltage is proportional to the integral of $J_y$ weighted by the coil sensitivity function. For the dominant mode:

$$V(t) = V_0 \cdot \exp(-t/\tau)$$

**Step 6:** Solve for wall thickness $d$ in terms of measured $\tau$:

$$d = \pi \sqrt{\frac{\tau}{\mu \sigma}}$$

**Step 7:** The relative change in $\tau$ due to a local wall thinning from $d$ to $(d - \Delta d)$ is:

$$\frac{\Delta \tau}{\tau} \approx \frac{2 \Delta d}{d} \quad \text{(for small $\Delta d$)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The time constant $\tau$ is **quadratically** proportional to the wall thickness. This means PEC is **highly sensitive** to wall loss — a 10% reduction in wall thickness produces approximately a 19% reduction in $\tau$. This is why PEC is excellent for detecting general corrosion, but has limited resolution for very small localized pits (which require higher-frequency ECT).

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| **Parameter** | **TSR** | **EMAE** | **PEC** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Physical Principle** | Heat diffusion | Electromagnetic + Acoustic | Electromagnetic induction |
| **Excitation Type** | Optical flash lamp | Pulsed/modulated current | Pulsed square wave current |
| **Detection Mechanism** | IR camera | Piezoelectric AE sensor | Pickup/sense coil |
| **Analysis Domain** | Time (log-log polynomial) | Time / Frequency | Time-domain decay |
| **Primary Application** | Subsurface defect depth profiling | Surface/near-surface cracks in conductors | Wall thickness & CUI detection |
| **Best Material** | Composites, metals, CFRP | Conductors (ferro & non-ferro) | Carbon steel, stainless steel, aluminum |
| **Penetration Depth** | 1–10 mm typical | Surface to 5 mm | Up to 100 mm (low freq) |
| **Sensitivity to Defect** | High for delamination & disbond | High for cracks | High for wall loss |
| **Contact Required** | No (non-contact IR) | Minimal / no contact | Minimal (lift-off tolerant) |
| **Speed** | Fast (1 frame = ms) | Moderate (kHz sampling) | Moderate (100 Hz–1 kHz PRR) |
| **KTU Module 3 Emphasis** | Post-processing math | Coupled-field physics | Time-domain NDT |
| **Cost** | Medium (camera + flash) | High (coils + AE + DAQ) | High (specialized probes) |

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — TSR Processing Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[StepA: Pulsed Heat Source] -->|Flash Pulse| B[StepB: Specimen Surface]
    B -->|Thermal Wave| C[StepC: Infrared Camera]
    C -->|Raw Thermal Image Sequence| D[StepD: Pixel-wise Time History]
    D -->|Convert to log domain| E[StepE: ln theta vs ln t]
    E -->|Polynomial Least Squares Fit| F[StepF: Fitted Coefficients a0 to an]
    F -->|Original Polynomial| G[StepG: Reconstructed Thermal Image]
    F -->|1st Derivative| H[StepH: 1st Derivative Image]
    F -->|2nd Derivative| I[StepI: 2nd Derivative Image]
    G --> J[StepJ: Defect Detection Output]
    H --> J
    I --> J
    J --> K[StepK: Forensic Engineering Report]
```

### Architecture Explanation
- **StepA → StepB:** The flash lamp delivers a uniform heat pulse of duration ~5 ms onto the specimen surface.
- **StepC:** The IR camera records the surface temperature decay at every pixel — typically 30–1000 frames per second.
- **StepD:** Each pixel produces a noisy temperature-time curve.
- **StepE:** The data is converted to log-log form to linearize the natural $1/\sqrt{t}$ decay.
- **StepF:** A polynomial of order $n$ (typically 7 or 8) is fitted to each pixel independently using least-squares.
- **StepG, StepH, StepI:** Three complementary image products are produced from the fitted coefficients.
- **StepJ:** Defects appear as bright or dark spots in these derivative images, depending on whether they cause faster or slower cooling.
- **StepK:** The forensic engineering report combines the three image products to characterize defect size, depth, and severity.

---

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — EMAE System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[StepA: Function Generator] -->|Pulsed or Modulated Waveform| B[StepB: Power Amplifier]
    B -->|High Current| C[StepC: Excitation Coil]
    C -->|Time-Varying Magnetic Field| D[StepD: Conductive Specimen]
    D -->|Induced Eddy Currents and Lorentz Forces| E[StepE: Elastic Wave Generation]
    E -->|Propagating Acoustic Wave| F[StepF: Piezoelectric AE Sensor]
    F -->|Electrical Signal| G[StepG: Preamplifier 40 dB]
    G -->|Amplified Signal| H[StepH: Bandpass Filter]
    H -->|Filtered Signal| I[StepI: Analog to Digital Converter]
    I -->|Digital Stream| J[StepJ: Signal Processing Unit]
    J -->|Feature Extraction| K[StepK: Defect Characterization]
    D -->|Defect Disrupts Field| L[StepL: Defect Region]
    L -->|Modified Acoustic Emission| E
```

### Architecture Explanation
- **StepA:** A function generator produces a pulsed or sinusoidal waveform (typically 1–10 kHz for the excitation current).
- **StepB:** A high-power amplifier (1 kW class) drives the coil to produce strong magnetic fields.
- **StepC:** The excitation coil is placed near (or around) the specimen.
- **StepD:** The changing magnetic field induces eddy currents in the conductive specimen.
- **StepE:** Lorentz forces (non-ferromagnetic) or magnetostriction (ferromagnetic) launches elastic waves.
- **StepF:** A wideband piezoelectric AE sensor (typically 150 kHz or 500 kHz resonant) detects the wave.
- **StepG → StepH → StepI:** Standard signal conditioning chain.
- **StepJ → StepK:** Time-frequency analysis (STFT, wavelet) extracts defect signatures.
- **StepL:** Defects locally modify the eddy current path and force distribution, producing characteristic signatures.

---

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — PEC Probe and Signal Path

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[StepA: Pulse Generator] -->|Square Wave Pulse| B[StepB: Drive Electronics]
    B -->|Current Pulse| C[StepC: Excitation Coil]
    C -->|Magnetic Field| D[StepD: Specimen with Wall]
    D -->|Induced Eddy Currents| E[StepE: Decaying Magnetic Field]
    E -->|Coupled Field| F[StepF: Pickup Sense Coil]
    F -->|Differential Voltage| G[StepG: Differential Amplifier]
    G -->|Conditioned Signal| H[StepH: ADC High Speed]
    H -->|Digital Samples| I[StepI: Time Domain Buffer]
    I -->|Analysis| J[StepJ: Early Time Lift-off Window]
    I -->|Analysis| K[StepK: Mid Time Thickness Window]
    I -->|Analysis| L[StepL: Late Time Deep Defect Window]
    J --> M[StepM: C-scan Image Reconstruction]
    K --> M
    L --> M
    M --> N[StepN: Forensic CUI Map]
```

### Architecture Explanation
- **StepA → StepC:** A current pulse (typically 1–10 ms wide) is delivered to the excitation coil.
- **StepD → StepE:** Eddy currents are induced in the specimen; they persist briefly after the pulse is switched off and decay exponentially.
- **StepF:** The pickup (sense) coil detects the decaying magnetic field from the eddy currents.
- **StepG → StepH:** The differential voltage is amplified and digitized at high sample rate (typically 100 kS/s to 1 MS/s).
- **StepI → StepL:** The digitized signal is partitioned into three time windows for different depth sensitivities.
- **StepM → StepN:** A C-scan image is built by plotting the chosen time-window amplitude (or $\tau$) as a function of probe position, producing a color-coded wall thickness / defect map.

---

## 4.4 Combined Triad — How TSR, EMAE, and PEC Complement Each Other

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[StepA: TSR Module] -->|Subsurface thermal map| D[StepD: Forensic Engineering Decision]
    B[StepB: EMAE Module] -->|Crack acoustic map| D
    C[StepC: PEC Module] -->|Wall thickness map| D
    D --> E[StepE: Integrated Defect Assessment]
    E --> F[StepF: Repair or Replace Recommendation]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Note:** In real-world forensic investigations of, e.g., an aging aircraft wing, an offshore platform, or a chemical reactor, all three techniques are often used **sequentially**:
> 1. **PEC** first to map general wall thinning and identify suspect regions (fast area scan).
> 2. **TSR** second to confirm whether suspect regions have subsurface delamination or hidden voids.
> 3. **EMAE** third to characterize the precise nature of any cracks (length, depth, orientation) with high resolution.
>
> This staged approach minimizes inspection time and cost while maximizing diagnostic confidence.
---
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]**
**Define Thermographic Signal Reconstruction (TSR). What is its principal purpose in non-destructive testing?**

#### Model Answer (3 Marks)
TSR is a **post-processing mathematical technique** applied to pulsed thermography data, originally developed by **Stephen M. Shepard (NASA)**. It fits a **low-order logarithmic polynomial** (typically 7th or 8th order) to the temperature-time history of every pixel in the thermal image sequence. Its **principal purpose** is to:
- **Reduce noise** in the raw thermographic data (improve SNR),
- Generate **1st and 2nd time derivative images** that highlight subsurface defects with superior contrast,
- Enable **quantitative depth profiling** of defects in composites and laminates.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** Students frequently write that TSR is a *heating* technique or a *new inspection method*. **This is wrong — TSR is a purely *software-based post-processing* technique.** The examiner specifically deducts 1 mark for this confusion. Always emphasize "post-processing" and "polynomial fitting" in your answer.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Question]**
**Briefly explain the principle of Pulsed Eddy Current (PEC) testing. Why is it preferred over conventional single-frequency Eddy Current Testing for Corrosion Under Insulation (CUI) applications?**

#### Model Answer (3 Marks)
PEC uses a **broadband pulsed excitation** (typically a square wave of 1–10 ms duration) instead of a single-frequency sinusoidal current. Because a single pulse contains a **broad spectrum of frequencies**, PEC can simultaneously probe **multiple depths** in a single scan. The response is analyzed in the **time domain**, with the **decay time constant** $\tau$ being directly related to wall thickness $d$:

$$\tau \approx \frac{\mu \sigma d^2}{\pi^2}$$

PEC is preferred over conventional ECT for **CUI** because:
- It can tolerate **large lift-offs** (up to 100 mm), so it works **through insulation** without stripping it,
- The **low-frequency components** in the pulse penetrate deep enough to measure **full wall thickness** of typical process pipes,
- It provides **quantitative wall thickness** information suitable for fitness-for-service assessment.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** Do not confuse PEC with conventional ECT. The examiner looks for the keywords "pulsed / broadband / time-domain / lift-off tolerant." A vague answer about "eddy currents" without these keywords will fetch only 1 out of 3 marks.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted to 2024 Scheme]**
**a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **principle and operational steps of Thermographic Signal Reconstruction (TSR)**. Discuss the significance of generating the **1st and 2nd time derivative images**. **(7 Marks)**

**b)** Explain the **physical mechanism of Electromagnetically Induced Acoustic Emission (EMAE)**. Distinguish clearly between the **Lorentz force mechanism** (for non-ferromagnetic materials) and the **magnetostrictive mechanism** (for ferromagnetic materials), with relevant equations. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Principle (2 marks):**
TSR is a post-processing algorithm that fits a logarithmic polynomial to thermal decay data of every pixel in a pulsed thermography image sequence. It was developed by S. M. Shepard at NASA to improve the signal-to-noise ratio and defect visibility.

**Operational Steps (3 marks):**
1. A uniform heat pulse (from a flash lamp) is applied to the specimen surface.
2. An IR camera records the surface temperature decay at every pixel over time.
3. For each pixel, the temperature-time history is converted to log-log form: $\ln(\theta)$ vs $\ln(t)$.
4. An n-th order polynomial (n = 7 or 8) is fitted to the log-transformed data using least-squares regression, producing coefficients $a_0, a_1, \ldots, a_n$.
5. The fitted polynomial is used to reconstruct a noise-free thermal image.
6. The 1st and 2nd time derivatives of the polynomial with respect to $\ln(t)$ are computed to generate derivative images.

**Significance of Derivative Images (2 marks):**
- **1st Derivative Image** — Represents the cooling rate. It sharpens the edges of defects and reduces the lateral thermal blur inherent in raw images. It is most effective for **near-surface defects**.
- **2nd Derivative Image** — Represents the cooling acceleration (the "rate of change of cooling rate"). It **maximizes the contrast** of **deeper, more subtle defects** that are otherwise invisible. The 2nd derivative amplifies the small slope changes caused by thermal barriers at defect boundaries.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Basic Principle (1 mark):**
EMAE combines electromagnetic induction with acoustic emission. A time-varying magnetic field (from a coil) induces eddy currents in the specimen; the interaction of these eddy currents with the magnetic field produces mechanical forces that launch elastic (acoustic) waves, which are detected by piezoelectric sensors.

**Lorentz Force Mechanism (3 marks):**
Applicable to **non-ferromagnetic conductors** (aluminum, copper, austenitic stainless steel). The induced eddy current density $\mathbf{J_e}$ interacts with the magnetic field $\mathbf{B}$ to produce a force per unit volume:

$$\mathbf{F_L} = \mathbf{J_e} \times \mathbf{B}$$

This force oscillates at the same frequency as the excitation current, launching elastic waves in the material. The wave frequency equals the excitation frequency. Defects disrupt the eddy current path, altering the local force and producing detectable changes in the received acoustic signal.

**Magnetostrictive Mechanism (3 marks):**
Applicable to **ferromagnetic materials** (carbon steel, iron, nickel). In these materials, a magnetic field produces a **dimensional change** in the crystal lattice called **magnetostriction**. The strain $S$ is a function of the applied field $H$, and for small variations:

$$\Delta S = d \cdot \Delta H$$

where $d$ is the magnetostrictive coefficient. This direct conversion of magnetic energy to mechanical strain is the source of elastic wave generation. The magnetostrictive mechanism is generally **stronger** than the Lorentz force mechanism in ferromagnetic materials.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** In part (b), students frequently forget to **name the two mechanisms explicitly** and instead write a generic answer. The examiner allocates **3 marks for Lorentz force** and **3 marks for magnetostrictive** — failure to address both mechanisms separately will cost up to **4 marks**. Also, do not confuse EMAE with EMAT — EMAE is the **emission** (passive) technique, while EMAT is the **transmission** (active) technique.

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Adapted to 2024 Scheme]**
**a)** Derive the **theoretical logarithmic polynomial model** of surface temperature decay used in TSR. Show clearly how the **higher-order polynomial coefficients** become non-zero in the presence of subsurface defects. **(7 Marks)**

**b)** Explain the **principle, instrumentation, and time-domain analysis of Pulsed Eddy Current (PEC) testing**. Discuss the role of the **time constant** $\tau$ in wall thickness measurement. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Derivation of the Logarithmic Polynomial (5 marks):**
For a semi-infinite solid subjected to an instantaneous heat pulse of energy $Q$ per unit area, the surface temperature response is:

$$\theta(t) = \frac{Q}{e\sqrt{\pi t}}$$

Taking the natural logarithm of both sides:

$$\ln[\theta(t)] = \ln(Q) - \ln(e) - \frac{1}{2}\ln(\pi) - \frac{1}{2}\ln(t)$$

Grouping the constant terms:

$$\ln[\theta(t)] = a_0 + a_1 \ln(t)$$

where $a_0 = \ln(Q) - \ln(e) - \frac{1}{2}\ln(\pi)$ and $a_1 = -1/2$.

For a **real defective specimen**, the response deviates from the ideal $1/\sqrt{t}$ behavior. The generalized form is:

$$\ln[\theta(t)] = a_0 + a_1 \ln(t) + a_2 [\ln(t)]^2 + a_3 [\ln(t)]^3 + \cdots + a_n [\ln(t)]^n$$

**Role of Higher-Order Coefficients (2 marks):**
In a defect-free area, $a_2 = a_3 = \ldots = a_n = 0$. When a subsurface defect is present, the defect acts as a **thermal barrier** (typically lower thermal conductivity than the host material, or an air gap with effectively zero conductivity). This causes a **change in the local cooling rate** that deviates from the ideal $1/\sqrt{t}$ behavior, which is mathematically expressed by the activation of the higher-order coefficients. The **magnitude of $a_2, a_3, \ldots, a_n$ is proportional to the size and depth of the defect**. The 2nd derivative of the polynomial (which depends on $a_2, a_3, \ldots$) therefore serves as an **amplified defect indicator**.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Principle (2 marks):**
PEC uses a **broadband pulsed excitation current** (typically a square wave of 1–10 ms duration) in the excitation coil. By Fourier theory, this single pulse contains a broad spectrum of frequencies. Low-frequency components penetrate deep; high-frequency components probe the surface. The eddy current response is monitored in the **time domain** after the pulse is switched off.

**Instrumentation (2 marks):**
- **Pulse generator and drive electronics** — Produce the current pulse.
- **Excitation coil (transmitter)** — Generates the pulsed magnetic field.
- **Pickup/sense coil (receiver)** — Detects the decaying magnetic field from the eddy currents.
- **Differential amplifier and ADC** — Amplify and digitize the sense coil signal.
- **Encoder for C-scan imaging** — Records probe position.

**Time-Domain Analysis (2 marks):**
The detected voltage decays exponentially:

$$V(t) = V_0 \cdot e^{-t/\tau}$$

The signal is divided into three time windows:
- **Early time** — Dominated by lift-off (probe-to-specimen gap).
- **Mid time** — Sensitive to wall thickness.
- **Late time** — Sensitive to deep defects and far-side features.

**Role of Time Constant in Wall Thickness (1 mark):**
The time constant $\tau$ is related to wall thickness $d$ by:

$$\tau \approx \frac{\mu \sigma d^2}{\pi^2}$$

This **quadratic relationship** means $\tau$ is highly sensitive to wall loss. By measuring $\tau$, the **absolute wall thickness** can be computed, making PEC ideal for **Corrosion Under Insulation (CUI)** mapping without insulation removal.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning for Question B:**
> - In part (a), students commonly skip the **intermediate step** of taking the logarithm. The examiner awards 2 marks specifically for the step "take ln of both sides." Skipping this loses 2 marks.
> - In part (b), students often forget the **three time windows** (early/mid/late) — this is a 2-mark item. Also, the **quadratic dependence of $\tau$ on $d$** is a frequently tested insight worth 1 mark.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for KTU Module 3:**

### TSR — Key Takeaways
- TSR is a **post-processing technique**, NOT a new inspection method.
- Developed by **Stephen M. Shepard (NASA)**.
- Fits a **logarithmic polynomial** of order **7 or 8** to pixel-wise decay data.
- Generates three image products: **Raw, 1st Derivative, 2nd Derivative**.
- **1st Derivative** = cooling rate → best for **near-surface defects**.
- **2nd Derivative** = cooling acceleration → best for **deep, subtle defects**.
- Higher-order polynomial coefficients ($a_2, a_3, \ldots$) are **non-zero only in defective regions**.
- Works best on **thin laminates and composites** (1D heat flow assumption).

### EMAE — Key Takeaways
- **EMAE** = Electromagnetic Induction + Acoustic Emission.
- Two mechanisms: **Lorentz force** (non-ferromagnetic) and **Magnetostriction** (ferromagnetic).
- Lorentz force equation: $\mathbf{F_L} = \mathbf{J_e} \times \mathbf{B}$.
- Magnetostrictive strain: $\Delta S = d \cdot \Delta H$.
- Acoustic wave frequency **equals** excitation current frequency.
- Detected by **wideband piezoelectric AE sensors** (e.g., 150 kHz or 500 kHz).
- Use cases: **SCC in austenitic stainless steel pipelines, weld HAZ cracks**.

### PEC — Key Takeaways
- Uses **broadband pulsed excitation** (1–10 ms pulses at 100 Hz – 1 kHz PRR).
- Analyzed in the **time domain** — three windows: **early (lift-off), mid (thickness), late (deep defects)**.
- Time constant: $\tau \approx \mu \sigma d^2 / \pi^2$ (quadratic in wall thickness).
- Tolerates **lift-off up to 100 mm** — works **through insulation**.
- **Industry gold standard** for **Corrosion Under Insulation (CUI)**.
- Standard depth of penetration: $\delta = 1 / \sqrt{\pi f \mu \sigma}$.
- Wall thickness formula: $d = \pi \sqrt{\tau / (\mu \sigma)}$.

### Common Points Across All Three Techniques
- All are **non-contact or minimal-contact** methods (advantage over UT and RT).
- All are **electromagnetic or thermal in nature** — complement UT and RT.
- All require careful **calibration, baseline, and reference standards**.
- All generate **digital data suitable for AI/ML-based automated defect classification** (a growing research area in forensic engineering).
- In the **KTU 2024 Scheme**, these are **theory-only** topics — focus on **principles, equations, and applications**, NOT on experimental procedures.

### Quick-Fire Self-Test Questions
1. Q: Who developed TSR? A: **Stephen M. Shepard (NASA)**.
2. Q: What order polynomial is used in TSR? A: **7th or 8th order**.
3. Q: Which image product in TSR reveals deep defects best? A: **2nd time derivative image**.
4. Q: Which mechanism dominates in EMAE for carbon steel? A: **Magnetostrictive**.
5. Q: Which mechanism dominates in EMAE for aluminum? A: **Lorentz force**.
6. Q: What is the time constant formula in PEC? A: $\tau = \mu \sigma d^2 / \pi^2$.
7. Q: Why is PEC preferred for CUI? A: **Lift-off tolerant, works through insulation**.
8. Q: What does EMAE stand for? A: **Electromagnetically Induced Acoustic Emission**.
9. Q: What is the typical pulse width in PEC? A: **1–10 ms**.
10. Q: What is the maximum practical lift-off in PEC? A: **Up to 100 mm**.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Engineer in the Court Room & Criminal Cases
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECET724 - FORENSIC ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Engineer in the Court Room & Criminal Cases

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Engineer in the Court Room — Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology):**
> An **Engineer in the Court Room** refers to the professional role assumed by a qualified engineering expert who is called upon by the judicial system to provide **specialized technical knowledge**, **scientific analysis**, and **expert opinion** on engineering matters that are beyond the common understanding of the judge and jury. This role transforms the engineer from a designer or constructor into a **Forensic Expert Witness** who assists the court in arriving at a just decision.

In the Indian judicial system (governed by the **Indian Evidence Act, 1872** and the **Bharatiya Sakshya Adhiniyam, 2023**), an engineer may appear in court under two principal capacities:

| Capacity | Definition | Purpose |
|----------|------------|---------|
| **Expert Witness** | A person possessing **special skill, knowledge, or experience** in a particular field (Section 45, Indian Evidence Act) | To provide **opinion evidence** on technical matters |
| **Fact Witness** | A person who has **direct personal knowledge** of the incident | To testify about **observed facts** without opinion |

---

## 1.2 Criminal Cases — Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:**
> A **Criminal Case** is a legal proceeding initiated by the **State (Government)** against an individual or organization accused of committing an act or omission that is punishable under criminal law (e.g., **Indian Penal Code, 1860** / **Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023**). In forensic engineering, criminal cases typically arise from **structural collapses, fire incidents, industrial accidents, vehicular accidents, product failures, and environmental disasters** where negligence, culpable homicide, or mischief is alleged.

---

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Plain English Analogy — The "Doctor vs. Engineer" Parallel:**
> Think of a courtroom as a hospital and the judge/jury as the patient. Just as a **doctor** diagnoses a disease using medical expertise that a layperson cannot understand, an **engineer** diagnoses the "disease" of a structural failure or accident using engineering principles. The court cannot "see" stress concentrations, fatigue cracks, or material degradation with the naked eye — the engineer becomes the **"translator"** between the physical evidence and the legal language of the court.
>
